text: story cards and the stories to be included in a release are determined by the time available and their relative priority. The developers break these stories into development ‘Tasks’. See Figures 3.5 and 3.6. Small releases The minimal useful set of functionality that provides business value is developed first. Releases of the system are frequent and incrementally add functionality to the first release. Simple design Enough design is carried out to meet the current requirements and no more. Test-first development An automated unit test framework is used to write tests for a new piece of functionality before that functionality itself is implemented. Refactoring* All developers are expected to refactor the code continuously as soon as possible code improvements are found. This keeps the code simple and maintainable. 30/10/2014 Extreme programming practices (b) Chapter 3 Agile Software Development 47 Pair programming Developers work in pairs, checking each other’s work and providing the support to always do a good job. Collective ownership The pairs of developers work on all areas of the system, so that no islands of expertise develop and all the developers take responsibility for all of the code. Anyone can change anything. Continuous integration As soon as the work on a task is complete, it is integrated into the whole system. After any such integration, all the unit tests in the system must pass. Sustainable pace Large amounts of overtime are not considered acceptable as the net effect is often to reduce code quality and medium term productivity On-site customer A representative of the end-user of the system (the customer) should be available full time for the use of the XP team. In an extreme programming process, the customer is a member of the development team and is responsible for bringing system requirements to the team for implementation. 30/10/2014 Practices of eXtreme Programming • 13 practices of XP • Whole Team • Metaphor • Planning game • Simple design • Small releases • Customer tests • Pair programming • Test-driven development • Design improvement • Collective code ownership • Continuous integration • Sustainable pace • Coding standards Whole Team • Remove artificial barrier between customer and development team • Client becomes a part of the team • Client makes critical decisions on the direction of the development team • Win - Win Important Simple Design • Design is always as simple as the current level of functionality allows • NO extraneous complexity needed or allowed • Keep the incremental design simple • Design must extend only far enough • Must encompass the next iteration’s added features • When code becomes cumbersome, it’s time for refactoring Important Small Releases • Tested & working code, whose functionality is specified by the client is very frequently released • For every iteration, the client gets a new software • Client evaluates & use the current status to dictate the content of the next delivery Important Collective Code Ownership • Team as a whole owns the system software (use code reviews to achieve this…) • Who originally wrote the code is most likely not remembered • Anyone can modify any code at any time without asking for permission • Revision control is definitely necessary • With XP, you shouldn’t expect your code to be in the same shape you left it and anything you

Their role is provide and prioritize new system requirements and to evaluate the iterations of the system. Incremental delivery The software is developed in increments with the customer specifying the requirements to be included in each increment. People not process The skills of the development team should be recognized and exploited. Team members should be left to develop their own ways of working without prescriptive processes. Embrace change Expect the system requirements to change and so design the system to accommodate these changes. Maintain simplicity Focus on simplicity in both the software being developed and in the development process. Wherever possible, actively work to eliminate complexity from the system. 30/10/2014 Agile method applicability • Product development where a software company is developing a small or medium-sized product for sale. • Virtually all software products and apps are now developed using an agile approach • Custom system development within an organization, where there is a clear commitment from the customer to become involved in the development process and where there are few external rules and regulations that affect the software. text: Software Engineering CSC648/848 Week 7 Summary 1. What is Coding/Programing Style? Set of rules or guidelines used when writing the source code for a computer program (Software) 2. What are the elements of coding style? 1. Naming Conventions (variables, functions, classes) 2. File naming and Organization 3. Formatting 4. Comments 5. Documentation 3. What are the benefits of good coding style? Easier: Maintenance, Testing, Optimization, and Portability. 4. What are some of the best practices of coding style for Security? 1. Use code & architecture reviews 2. Check inputs 3. Ensure proper transmission and management of Passwords 4. Use secure internet protocols 5. Check buffer size 6. Prevent injection of SQL code into forms Henry VILLAR SECURITY ENGINEERING Software Systems that can resist malicious attacks Security SW Engineering • Security engineering focuses on how to develop and maintain software systems that can resist malicious attacks intended to damage a computer-based system or its data. 4 Important Objective and Topics Objective: Cover SE processes and practices related to creating secure SW systems. • Security concepts, terminology • Security Requirement and Specification Process • Security Risk management • Design and coding practices for security • Some practical advices • Security certificates and HTTPS for WWW sites • Securing forms input 6 Issues • As in all complex systems security management relates to ❑People ❑Process, policies ❑Technology 7 Important Check current press • Frequent coverage of security breaches (often simply unprotected PW) • Latest: • https://www.splunk.com/en_us/cyb er-security/solarwinds-cyberattack- response.html • https://healthitsecurity.com/news/w hite-house-solarwinds-hack- impacted-9-fed-agencies-100- entities • https://cybersecuritynews.com/cyber- security-news-letter-october/ • https://www.cisa.gov/topics/cyber- threats-and-advisories • Good career path 8 System layers where security may be compromised Chapter 13 Security Engineering 9 Security concepts/terminology Term Definition Asset Something of value which has to be protected. The asset may be the software system itself or data used by that system. Attack An exploitation of a system’s vulnerability. Generally, this is from outside the system and is a deliberate attempt to cause some damage. Control A protective measure that reduces a system’s vulnerability. Encryption is an example of a control that reduces a vulnerability of a weak access control system Exposure Possible loss or harm to a computing system. This can be loss or damage to data, or can be a loss of time and effort if recovery is necessary after a security breach. Threat Circumstances that have potential to cause loss or harm. You can think of these as a system vulnerability that is subjected to an attack. Vulnerability A weakness in a computer-based system that may be exploited to cause loss or harm. 10 Chapter 13 Security Engineering Important Security • The security of a system is a system property that reflects the system’s ability to protect itself from accidental or deliberate external attack. • Security is essential as most systems are networked so that external access to the system through the Internet is possible. • Security is an essential pre-requisite for availability, reliability and safety. 12 Chapter 13 Security Engineering Security dimensions - Types of Threats • Confidentiality • Information in a system may be disclosed or made accessible to people or programs that are not authorized to have access to that information. • Integrity • Information in a system may be damaged or corrupted making it unusual or unreliable. • Availability • Access to a system or

chunk_id: SFSU CSC648-848 [02] Fall 2024 C8 Security Engineering (s)-1
text: system through the Internet is possible. • Security is an essential pre-requisite for availability, reliability and safety. 12 Chapter 13 Security Engineering Security dimensions - Types of Threats • Confidentiality • Information in a system may be disclosed or made accessible to people or programs that are not authorized to have access to that information. • Integrity • Information in a system may be damaged or corrupted making it unusual or unreliable. • Availability • Access to a system or its data that is normally available may not be possible. 12/11/2014 Chapter 13 Security Engineering 13 Security Assurance - Controls against threats • Vulnerability avoidance • The system is designed so that vulnerabilities do not occur. For example, if there is no external network connection then external attack is impossible • Attack detection and elimination • The system is designed so that attacks on vulnerabilities are detected and neutralised before they result in an exposure. For example, virus checkers find and remove viruses before they infect a system • Exposure limitation and recovery • The system is designed so that the adverse consequences of a successful attack are minimised. For example, a backup policy allows damaged information to be restored 14 Chapter 13 Security Engineering 12/11/2014 Security requirements and specification process Acknowledged use of SE book by Sommerville (2006), 8e 15 The preliminary risk assessment process for security requirements 16 Chapter 13 Security Engineering 12/11/2014 Important Security Risk Management Acknowledged use of SE book by Sommerville (2006), 8e 17 Overview • Assessing the possible losses that might ensue from attacks on the system • Balancing these losses against the costs of security procedures that may reduce these losses. • Risk management should be driven by an organizational security policy. • Risk management involves • Preliminary risk assessment • Life cycle risk assessment Acknowledged use of SE book by Sommerville (2006), 8e 18 Important Asset Analysis: Example Asset Value Exposure The information system High High, Financial loss – losing customers, restoring system, communication The user database High High, Financial loss, harm to users An individual user record Normally low other than high profile patients Direct loss is low, loss of reputation Acknowledged use of SE book by Sommerville (2006), 8e 19 Threat and Control Analysis: Example Threat Probability Control Feasibility Unauthorized user gains access to confidential data High 1. Require users to authenticate themselves. 2. Track system usage High cost, user resistance. Simple to implement, supports recovery. Unauthorized user makes system unavailable Low Allow system management from known physical location that are secure Low cost. Attention to distribution of keys. Acknowledged use of SE book by Sommerville (2006), 8e 20 Security Design and Coding Guidelines Acknowledged use of SE book by Sommerville (2006), 8e 21 Secure systems design • Security should be designed into a system – it is very difficult to make an insecure system secure after it has been designed or implemented • Architectural design • how do architectural design decisions affect the security of a system? • Good practice • what is accepted good practice when designing secure systems? Chapter 13 Security Engineering 22 12/11/2014 Design and risk assessment Chapter 13 Security Engineering 23 12/11/2014 Architectural Design • Protection • How should the system be organized so that critical assets can be protected against external attack? • Distribution

Architecture, Code, github, team account, database and general SW and code review will be done off-line. • NOTE: The M3 SUMMARY document must be part of the M5 final documentation Final Project Main Functions • Web App: Voting Guide with AI+ 1. Search & Filter by state, political party and issues/policies. 2. Enter official information (political parties, voter registration, polling location, voting process, etc.) 3. Site Registration (College Students, General public, Officials, Admin, etc.). 4. Rate candidates. 5. Search results may be displayed using maps 6. AI capabilities is a plus. Two entrepreneurs, SFSU alumni, decided to create a start-up comprising of a team of about 6+ students to develop a web- based service to help USA college students and general public to identify county, state, and federal/national candidates running for public offices and their respective policies, and background. The web application will allow officials from the different political parties to enter the corresponding official information, and the Elections office to enter information about voter registration, polling locations, and voting process. College students and general public should be able to search by state, political party and issues/policies so results can list the corresponding data, and at the same time; user (student and general public) can enter their issues of interest and the AI chatbot can assist them with mapping what candidates are aligned with the user interest. This application is targeting three primary audiences: (1) College Students, (2) General Public, and (3) Officials from the political parties and election office. Students and general public users should be able to rate candidates. Officials from political parties and election office may need to be registered to use all the functionality of the application. General users may access to limited search capabilities and application functionality. Search results may display maps. Thank You

chunk_id: SFSU CSC648-848 [02] Fall 2024 C9 Software QA-0
text: Software Engineering CSC648/848 SW Engineering Process Components 1. Requirements and Specification 2. Design and Implementation 3. Validation 4. Maintenance and Evolution Henry VILLAR SW QUALITY ASSURANCE Testing and QA Remember CSC 639 Software Testing and Quality Assurance? • Software system quality components and activities that support software quality • QA objectives: reliability, correctness, testability, maintainability, flexibility, portability, efficiency, integrity, usability, reusability, and interoperability. • Theoretical background: program correctness proofs, cyclomatic complexity, software reliability modeling. • Software unit testing to verify unit specifications • Integration testing to verify design specifications • System testing to verify requirements specifications • Usability testing • Performance, reliability, and regression testing • Alpha, beta and acceptance testing • Software testing and quality assurance tools. Open source testing using NUnit and JUnit. • Software testing and QA project • Planning, organizing, and budgeting test procedures • Cost management of test projects: cost estimating, cost budgeting, project financing, cash flow management • Time management of test projects: schedule development, resource planning and allocation, network diagrams • Project quality management: standards, documentation, objectives, planning and control • Human resource management: QA project HR planning, staff acquisition and deployment, team building and training, team management • Managing outsourced QA projects: virtual team management, offshore team management Video https://youtu.be/xCwkjZcEK6w Testing of SW • QA –quality assurance or SW testing: in narrow definition means ensuring that program performs according to some specs (this does NOT mean the specs are correct nor that the SW is usable) ➔ focus of this class • Performing to specs: the program does what is specified AND it has no bugs. Note: it can have no bugs but still miss on specs Usability testing is different from QA, it is testing to ensure that the SW is usable for intended users, not to find bugs • Performance testing: program is tested for speed, load etc. Important Difference between usability testing and QA • Usability testing: check if the SW is easy to use 1. Give user a task to do describing only WHAT to do not HOW to do it (“find an emergency situation in one of the counties in California”) 2. Collect input on how user feels after doing the task (“finding an extreme weather condition was easy” – strongly agree | x | x | x | strongly disagree) • QA testing: check if SW performs to specs 1. Give user very specific tasks on WHAT to do (“enter/select ‘Health’ in search field and verify that you get COVID or health infections result items located in specific counties in California” 2. Tester reports PASS or FAIL Important 8 More on definitions • SW testing (often called QA) – ensuring SW performs to specs. Narrower than SQA • http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Software_testing • SW Quality Assurance (SQA) is broader that SW testing and refers to the whole SE process • http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Software_Quality_Assurance • SW Quality Control (SQC) involves checking all the SE documentation (requirements, specs etc.) – refers to the product while SQA refers to the process Important Levels of Software Testing Most important types of testing • Functionality testing – make sure functions do what they are supposed to do • Unit testing – done by individual programmers before checking in • Smoke testing – quick automated test (used as part of daily build and test) • Comprehensive or
source_file: SFSU CSC648-848 [02] Fall 2024 C9 Software QA.pdf

chunk_id: SFSU CSC648-848 [02] Fall 2024 C9 Software QA-1
text: • http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Software_Quality_Assurance • SW Quality Control (SQC) involves checking all the SE documentation (requirements, specs etc.) – refers to the product while SQA refers to the process Important Levels of Software Testing Most important types of testing • Functionality testing – make sure functions do what they are supposed to do • Unit testing – done by individual programmers before checking in • Smoke testing – quick automated test (used as part of daily build and test) • Comprehensive or regression testing – longer, automated, does complete test • Cross platform testing – browsers, OSes, libraries • Usability testing – a) General usability; b) UI functionality • Load/performance testing – how does the system perform under load (applications, users, data size etc.) • Security testing – can system withstand malicious attacks and code • Certification/compliance testing –in certain mission critical areas Important Most important types of testing • Unit testing: tests individual developer performs on the “unit” he she developed before submission (can be automated too) • Smoke testing: quick test (often done daily as part of automated build and test) to quickly check if the system is in reasonably good state (compiles, builds, runs, performs basic tasks) • Comprehensive test: tests all functions, may take long time • Regression testing: used repetitively to test the whole system after some changes were made • Integration testing: used when code is developed by many teams, must budget time to test integrated code after it has been tested as a unit. Important for Open Source and in global SE Important A model of the software testing process Chapter 8 Software Testing Important Kinds of bugs • Bugs happen repetitively, upon same conditions – repetitive bugs (easy to diagnose…) • Bugs happen from time to time – conditions not clear – intermittent bugs (hard to diagnose) • System does not conform to some standard – certification testing • System is too slow – performance testing • System gives different results in different OSes – cross platform compatibility testing • UI behaves differently for different browsers - cross browser compatibility testing • Security bugs/exposures – enable malicious attacks and code to cause damage and security breaches – security testing Important Types of bugs - behavior • Repeatable: always happen when certain specs are followed (easier to diagnose) • Intermittent: one can not find consistent ways to generate the bug (hard to diagnose) • Performance: way out of specified performance • Racing/timing: system works with slow input, but not with fast input or speed Important Bug management • Bugs need to be categorized, prioritized and tracked (besides being squashed) • Agree and communicate bug categories to all (QA and Development) • Bug severity categories – external view of bug’s overall impact • Critical, serious, minor • Bug priority – when they need to be dealt with • Test blocker, show stopper, beta stopper, release stopper, regular, deferred • Have an easy to use and consistent system accessible to Development and QA Important Classifying Bugs – Severity - Impact on system performance • CRITICAL • crash, halt, hang • loss of data • major functionality missing / in error • security violations • SERIOUS • performance issues • functionality • many migration issues • ease of use • MINOR • spelling errors • minor functionality issues

chunk_id: SFSU CSC648-848 [02] Fall 2024 C9 Software QA-2
text: with • Test blocker, show stopper, beta stopper, release stopper, regular, deferred • Have an easy to use and consistent system accessible to Development and QA Important Classifying Bugs – Severity - Impact on system performance • CRITICAL • crash, halt, hang • loss of data • major functionality missing / in error • security violations • SERIOUS • performance issues • functionality • many migration issues • ease of use • MINOR • spelling errors • minor functionality issues Important Classifying Bugs – Priority – when the bug needs to be fixed • Test Blocker – tests can not be done until fixed • Show Stopper – the whole product in jeopardy • Beta Stopper – must fix before beta is launched • Release Stopper - must fix before release • Post-release – OK to fix after release • Deferred – do it later • Document – (and don’t fix) Important Testing strategies • Black box • No knowledge of internals. • Test plans derived from specs and requirements • Testing done by running the executable, no view of the source code • White box • Access to source code is available • Test derived from specs and also from inspecting the code (e.g. problem code is tested) • Test done by running the code and inspecting the source code Important Testing strategies • Developers do unit testing of their module locally, on their system (necessary but not sufficient) • Code reviewed before checking in • Developers optionally can add test suggestions for QA • Code integrated, QA tests the code again as well as the whole system - integration testing • Integrated system rebuilt and smoke tested often – build and smoke test Testing strategies • How to maximize coverage with minimal number of test sequences • Partition inputs into equivalence sets or classes – each class member executes single path, so only a few elements of each class need to be used for testing • Exhaustive testing: test all combinations of inputs and conditions (very expensive, possible only in some cases) • Path testing: test all paths • Code coverage: cover all code or all key code • Integration testing: Test modules independently, then test full system by incrementally adding modules Important Test Plan – outline 1. Test objectives: what is tested and what is not tested 2. Setup of HW 3. Setup of SW including at what SW state the testing beings 4. Test environment, human resources needs, expected time to complete 5. Risks, contingencies 6. End report format requirements 7. Actual test sequence, test input AND test expected results 8. Final report Important Examples of WRONG test plan • Input: enter “red” • Output : Check that results are correct • Input: enter some input • Output : Check that results are correct • Input: enter “red” • Output : Check that ALL results contain “red” Important Examples of GOOD test plan • Input: enter “red” • Output : Check that you get 5 results, all containing string “red” in name field • Input: enter “red” • Output : Check that results consist of exactly these • Result 1 • Result 2 • -… • Result 5 Important Report of tests – make them easy to read (there are a lot of test cases) •

text: enter “red” • Output : Check that ALL results contain “red” Important Examples of GOOD test plan • Input: enter “red” • Output : Check that you get 5 results, all containing string “red” in name field • Input: enter “red” • Output : Check that results consist of exactly these • Result 1 • Result 2 • -… • Result 5 Important Report of tests – make them easy to read (there are a lot of test cases) • Tabular format Number Description Test input Expected output PASS/FAIL 1 Test % like In search for name field “red” Get 5 results, all have “red” in name field Important Make this specific How to present QA status and results • Graphs for QA progress reports • Number of open bugs in time • Number of bugs in time grouped by area • Number of bugs in time grouped by severity/priority • Critical in making product release decisions • Has to be easy to understand to non-CS management Important Usual testing sequence Daily or very often 1. Developers do unit testing 2. Submit code for review 3. Reviewed code OKed (or fixed) and submitted to source control 4. Daily “smoke test” or full test done 5. a) move on to next submissions or b) lock submissions and fix problems from last working build QA department performs scheduled full tests New tools like Jenkins, Travis can integrate these steps Important Unit testing tools • One example: junit • http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/JUnit • http://junit.org/ • Can write your own • Relates to “test driven development” – write test plan before coding, then test it Smoke test • Efficient test that can be applied quickly, in an automated way, to do some basic test (system compiles, files can be stored and retrieved etc.) • Intended to discover obvious bugs and problems quickly • Used as part of “build and test” process to be applied often (even daily) • By having this, one limits number of possible bugs to a small number (with high probability only since the last some and test) • Make it as comprehensive as efficiently allows, automate and integrate with build system with automated report generation and possibly e-mail alerts • Critical for effective SW development Important How to test UI subsystems in QA context (adhering to specs, NOT for usability) • To test UI one has to try all the sequence of user actions to cover all the functions and “buttons” • Very time consuming ➔ automation tools are developed • UI test automation tools: • QA set up the system • QA trains the system manually by providing user input (e.g. clicks on buttons) and expected results • QA test tool records the training data above and repeats during the test • Test results are recorded and summarized Important M4-5 • Milestone 4. Rate candidates.

text: Milestone 4. Beta Launch, QA and Usability Testing and Final Commitment for Product Features (P1 list). November 12th 2024 (W12) • Milestone 5. Final Demo and Documents for Grading. December 10th 2024 (W15) Final Project Main Functions • Web App: Voting Guide with AI+ 1. Search & Filter by state, political party and issues/policies. 2. Enter official information (political parties, voter registration, polling location, voting process, etc.) 3. Site Registration (College Students, General public, Officials, Admin, etc.). 4. Rate candidates. 5. Search results may be displayed using maps 6. AI capabilities is a plus. Two entrepreneurs, SFSU alumni, decided to create a start-up comprising of a team of about 6+ students to develop a web- based service to help USA college students and general public to identify county, state, and federal/national candidates running for public offices and their respective policies, and background. The web application will allow officials from the different political parties to enter the corresponding official information, and the Elections office to enter information about voter registration, polling locations, and voting process. College students and general public should be able to search by state, political party and issues/policies so results can list the corresponding data, and at the same time; user (student and general public) can enter their issues of interest and the AI chatbot can assist them with mapping what candidates are aligned with the user interest. This application is targeting three primary audiences: (1) College Students, (2) General Public, and (3) Officials from the political parties and election office. Students and general public users should be able to rate candidates. Officials from political parties and election office may need to be registered to use all the functionality of the application. General users may access to limited search capabilities and application functionality. Search results may display maps. Study Materials Acknowledge • Content of these slides are based on course materials created by Dr Dragutin Petkovic 34 Some major problems happened due to poor SW QA • http://www.softwareqatest.com/qatfaq1.html#FAQ1_3 QA principles • Same principles and high level process apply to all platforms from servers to PCs to mobile • Differences are in specific implementaion, tools etc. 35 36 Bugs/errors examples • Specs not followed (function missing or function behaves not as to specs, otherwise application looks OK, no bugs) • System crashes • System hangs • Computation done wrong • System inconsistent – upon repeating the same steps one gets different results or behavior • System works initially but after a while shows problems • System works with manual input but not with programmatic (fast) input • System course problems with “corner cases” • System does not read/store required file formats • Loss of data 37 Bug management (2) • Use bug tracking systems to record/manage bugs: • Bug Severity • Bug Priority • Description • Assignment (who is to look into ti - determine from list of assigned developers) • Status (open, in progress, closed, deferred) • Have teams assigned to categorize and analyze bugs. Often, many bugs come from one single defect. Priorities and categories sometimes need to be addressed (and changed) by team of developers and QA • Bugs can be opened by anybody, closed only by QA • Late in product cycle, frequent (daily) meetings of bug teams (QA, development) Important 38 Bug management (3)

Priorities and categories sometimes need to be addressed (and changed) by team of developers and QA • Bugs can be opened by anybody, closed only by QA • Late in product cycle, frequent (daily) meetings of bug teams (QA, development) Important 38 Bug management (3) • Bug reports: • Total number of bugs in time (use charts to present) • Number of bugs in each category, priority, subsystem, developer (optional) • Analysis of causes, trends, critical issues (note: bugs can be correlated – some bug can cause many others…) • Final Go/NO GO on product release usually gated by QA report summarizing above statistics Important 39 Test strategies for SW • How to define and discover bugs Recall: QA job is to stress test the system and diagnose as many problems as possible with minimal resource/time The earlier problems are discovered, the cheaper it is 40 • Partition system inputs and outputs into ‘equivalence sets’ • If input is a 5-digit integer between 10,000 and 99,999, equivalence partitions are <10,000, 10,000-99, 999 and > 100, 000 • Choose test cases at the boundary of these sets • 00000, 09999, 10000, 99999, 100000, 100001 Test strategy 1: Equivalence partitioning From: Ian Sommerville Important 41 Equivalence partitions Between 10000 and 99999 Less than 10000 More than 99999 9999 10000 50000 100000 99999 Input values Between 4 and 10 Less than 4 More than 10 3 4 7 11 10 Number of input values From: Ian Sommerville 42 Examples from WWW forms • Specs: • User name – text entry. Validate that entered string has more than 1 characters • User phone – text entry. Validate that the string has at least 7 digits • User age – text entry. Validate that the value is greater or equal 10 and less than 100 • What are equivalence sets here? 43 • Key element in array • Key element not in array • Input array has a single value • Input array has an even number of values • Input array has an odd number of values Binary search - equiv. partitions Binary search (Java) class BinSearch { // This is an encapsulation of a binary search function that takes an array of // ordered objects and a key and returns an object with 2 attributes namely // index - the value of the array index // found - a boolean indicating whether or not the key is in the array // An object is returned because it is not possible in Java to pass basic types by // reference to a function and so return two values // the key is -1 if the element is not found public static void search ( int key, int [] elemArray, Result r ) { int bottom = 0 ; int top = elemArray.length - 1 ; int mid ; r.found = false ; r.index = -1 ; while ( bottom <= top ) { mid = (top + bottom) / 2 ; if (elemArray [mid] == key) { r.index = mid ; r.found = true ; return ; } //

1 ; } } //while loop } // search } //BinSearch Test strategy 2: White-box testing: Test cases derived Upon code inspection From: Ian Sommerville 45 Test strategy 3: Path testing • The objective of path testing is to ensure that the set of test cases is such that each path through the program is executed at least once • The starting point for path testing is a program flow graph that shows nodes representing program decisions and arcs representing the flow of control • Statements with conditions are therefore nodes in the flow graph From: Ian Sommerville Important 46 Test strategy 4: Integration testing • Goal: Tests complete systems or subsystems composed of integrated components • Critical when systems are build from components, by different teams (most often the case) • Each subsystem is first unit tested • Subsystems are then incrementally added to the system and tests are performed at each step Any considerable system should have well identified modules or subsystems ➔ it allows “divide and conquer” strategy in testing (and development) Important 47 Test Plans • QA generates test plans • Purpose: • Instructions for testers • Planning, resource allocation 48 Test sequence • Intent is to be clear and complete, and require minimal user experience (deferent from usability test plans) • Create test plan/sequence (e.g. instructions) with specific input and specific output so PASS/FAIL can be easily (and automatically) determined • Cover + and – cases and corner cases Examples of test sequences - search • Test sequence: “Input “red” and check output to be correct”– WRONG sequence/plan – why? • Correct approach • Establish “ground truth” e.g. test data ➔what is the specific correct output for certain specific input (also helps in Test Driven Development) • Use specific concrete cases for test plan/sequence 49 Examples of test sequences – search (2) • Assume your search is by CATEGORY (via pull down) and text entry (using %LIKE) • Create your own test DB • Item 1: category = FURNITURE”; description = “new red sofa” • Item 2: category = FURNITURE”; description = “new blue sofa” • Item 1: category = FURNACES”; description = “new red” • Item 1: category = FURNACES”; description = “goes with red furniture” • Develop test cases to test + and – and corner cases. Try to “break” the system by choosing corner cases that “stress the system” • Determine “by hand” correct answers • Use the above to determine test plan/sequence and expected correct output R 50 Examples of test sequences – search (3) • Some examples of corner cases for the test DB • Category = CARS • Category = FURNITURE text = “red” • Category = FURNITURE text

Use it! • Good for job search – many companies use it and require it.. Resources: Other relevant topics • Continuous integration and tools to autmate it – Jenkins, Travis • WWW testing • Resource leaks testing (e.gt. Memory leaks) • Mobile testing • Cross browser testing • Performance testing 55 Continuous Integration (CI) • Provides the link between committing and deploying. • Allows monitoring by management and tech leads • After commit to repo, Jenkins automatically runs tests: – If FAIL: Jenkins generates reports, rolls back codebase to last working version – If SUCCESS: Jenkins deploys to testing server – Status reported and logged –https://jenkins.io/ Continuous Integration (CI) • Example PHP tests that Jenkins would run: –PHP Unit - Unit tests for PHP code –PHP LOC - Measures lines of code, comments, lines in classes and methods –PHP Depend - Static code analysis on a given source base. –PHP Syntax checker • Can be configured & extended for other tests & behaviors Travis tool for continuous integration • https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Travis_CI “When Travis CI has been activated for a given repository, GitHub will notify it whenever new commits are pushed to that repository or a pull request is submitted. It can also be configured to only run for specific branches, or branches whose names match a specific pattern. Travis CI will then check out the relevant branch and run the commands specified in .travis.yml, which usually build the software and run any automated tests. When that process has completed, Travis notifies the developer(s) in the way it has been configured to do so[5]—for example, by sending an email containing the test results (showing success or failure), or by posting a message on an IRC channel. In the case of pull requests, the pull request will be annotated with the outcome and a link to the build log, using a GitHub integration.” 58 QA for Mobile platforms • Similar principles and polices, different tools • http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mobile-device_testing • http://developer.android.com/tools/help/monkey.html • http://www.vogella.com/tutorials/AndroidTesting/article.html • http://www.softwaretestinghelp.com/robotium-tutorial-android-application- ui-testing-tool/ 59 Important 60 Web Testing • Testing of Web applications • Wikipedia http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Web_testing • Functionality testing – make sure functions do what they are supposed to do • UI testing – a) usability; b) UI functionality • Load/performance testing – how does the system perform under load (applications, users, data size etc.) • Security testing – can system withstand malicious

text: QA for Mobile platforms • Similar principles and polices, different tools • http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mobile-device_testing • http://developer.android.com/tools/help/monkey.html • http://www.vogella.com/tutorials/AndroidTesting/article.html • http://www.softwaretestinghelp.com/robotium-tutorial-android-application- ui-testing-tool/ 59 Important 60 Web Testing • Testing of Web applications • Wikipedia http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Web_testing • Functionality testing – make sure functions do what they are supposed to do • UI testing – a) usability; b) UI functionality • Load/performance testing – how does the system perform under load (applications, users, data size etc.) • Security testing – can system withstand malicious attacks and code • Compatibility testing (cross browser support) Important 61 Cross platform testing and issues - PCs • Systems can behave differently and produce different results with different Oses (but produce no bugs) • Often, issues exist with floating point library, round of errors etc. – done differently in deferent OSes • Critical to test for numerical and scientific applications • Programming strategy and arch. have to enable portability • Need to test for this – do not assume it • Test matrix grows fast: # of OS X # of browsers • Use VMs or Cloud computing to efficiently cover combinations OS/browsers (more on cloud later) Important 62 Cross browser testing and issues • Remains a serous issue despite promises to the contrary (“program once, run on all browsers”) • 3-4 major variants • Need to support at least 2 versions of each • iPAD, mobile platforms also need to be addressed • Cross browser compatibility: • Web application has to work on all browsers (at least 3-4 major variants) and increasingly on mobile and iPADs • Web application has to create the same UI experience for different browsers Important Cross browser testing and issues (2) • Wikipedia • http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cross-browser • Exponentially adds to number of test cases • Use Web UI test automation • http://seleniumhq.org/ • http://www.froglogic.com/?gclid=CMGzuYWRpawCFQUaQgodTG1DDA • Strategies: • Adhere to cross browser compatibility in coding and design • Use simple functions and approaches in coding, they usually are easier to work on all platforms • Use latest tools to test your browsers (reviews are here • http://www.smashingmagazine.com/2011/08/07/a-dozen-cross-browser-testing- tools/ • http://slodive.com/web-development/cross-browser-testing/ ) • Mobile access – often needs different UI design and coding 63 Important Testing in Open Source world • Who wins? • Large companies with well established QA departments or • Open Source community which continually tests the code? • Open source wins in general • Since you will use many components from Open Source integration testing is the key plus white box testing – check the code too 64 Other types of testing 65 66 Testing for memory leaks, handle leaks, racing conditions (main cause of intermittent bugs) • Programmers often make mistakes in not releasing resources (memory, handles) • Systems can work when tested briefly, then slow down or hang up after long operation. Problems are hard to trace without tools – usually there are no obvious classic bugs. • Tools help point to problems • Valgrind http://valgrind.org/ • SIMPLE: Use Windows task manager or similar – run the program in the loop and observe memory usage • Use stress testing, preferably automated Important 67 Performance testing and tuning • Various tools (I.e. profilers) can help give an idea where the time is spent in the SW • Often, most time is spent in very few modules. One more reason to have

text: usually there are no obvious classic bugs. • Tools help point to problems • Valgrind http://valgrind.org/ • SIMPLE: Use Windows task manager or similar – run the program in the loop and observe memory usage • Use stress testing, preferably automated Important 67 Performance testing and tuning • Various tools (I.e. profilers) can help give an idea where the time is spent in the SW • Often, most time is spent in very few modules. One more reason to have the code well modularized ➔ you can easily focus your testing on key “suspects” • Extremely important in SW design and development: use on prototypes and final systems • Make sure the benchmark itself does not interfere with the application • Make sure system conditions and setups are realistic (memory, other processes running, network, caching, load time…) • Repeat measurements many times and average results • Develop and test first for correctness, then speed Important Copyright D. Petkovic 68 Performance measurements • Measure efficiency with given load (e.g. other applications, numbers of users) or system constraints (e.g. memory, network) • Refers to I/O, CPU, graphics, DB, throughput, number of users • Standard benchmarks are used in order to measure efficiency – they simulate realistic loads • Good benchmarks are run by independent companies, in carefully controlled and published settings • Benchmarks help compare apples with apples • Make sure to also test the actual application you have to provide to the user 69 Benchmarks – good resources and organizations • Valgrind http://valgrind.org/ • Standard Performance Evaluation Corp (SPEC) • http://www.specbench.org/ • TPC – Transaction performance Council (for databases) • http://www.tpc.org/ • Web benchmarking (user load, database connections etc…) • Jmeter http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/JMeter • LoadRunner – test for loads http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/LoadRunner 70 Security QA • Usually consists of: • White box testing of code (must see code, example: array bounds checking) (Note: good to follow “safe coding style” to prevent security problems…) • Audit of architecture: • Black box testing: trying to break it; • Fault injections • Audit of policies and procedures (e.g. password administration, personnel security checks, training, execution of polices etc.) • (Check slides on SE for Security) Important 71 Advanced topics – QA using Virtual machines • Testing using Virtual machines (I.e. VMware) solves several key problems: • Offers all HW/SW/OS/Browser combinations via SW provisioning • Easy to revert to initial state • Can run multiple virtual machines on one PC – speeds up QA tremendously • Can automate QA by 1) launching Virtual Machines (VM) with reconfigured test programs, 2) perform the tests in each VM, 3) collect the results • VMs from Open Source offerings, also www.vmware.com • Resources on use of VMs in QA: • https://www.howtogeek.com/206286/stop-testing-software-on-your-pc-use- virtual-machine-snapshots-instead/ • https://www.apriorit.com/qa-blog/223-virtualization-in-testing Important Using Cloud computing for QA • Similar to using VMs, but in this case you “rent” and “provision” all VMs via cloud. Cloud is provided and maintained by others and provides all combinations of virtual HW/SW/OS you need • Strongly recommended – much cheaper and faster compared to having the right VMs and HW • Resources: • http://www.softwaretestinghelp.com/getting-started-with-cloud- testing/ 72 Important 73 QA and outsourcing • Note successful outsourcing of some QA – allows for non stop development – E.G. build, compile in USA, send for test to India; next day check test results and continue

Enforce it. • Use source code management to number releases – helps in regression testing • Automate build and smoke test system into one, do it often, daily at the final stages of product cycle • Do full and regression testing often • Develop easy to read and comprehend test reports ➔ critical for making a decision on product launch 74 Important Future: QA in the era of on-line services and big data • Products not released is well orchestrated downloads but continuously available and modified (e.g. on-line services, on-line search) • QA is augmented by “test-in-production” where QA is being continuously done • Data scientists and ML experts analyze and discover/predict bugs 75 Important Great paper to read and discuss - M. Kim et all: Role of data scientist in SE teams”, ICSE 2016 • Paper • http://homepages.dcc.ufmg.br/~figueiredo/disciplinas/2016b/sqm/paper10laerte.pdf • Problems data scientists in SE work on: • Performance Regression • Requirements Identification • Fault Localization and Root Causes Analysis • Bug Prioritization • Server Anomaly Detection • Failure Rate Estimation • Customer Understanding • Cost Benefit Analysis 76 77 QA and Software Engineering, Teamwork and Process issues • Involve QA early, as soon as requirements and specs are done • QA gets trained and prepares resources • Establishes teamwork • Keep working closely with QA especially at the end of the cycle • Let developers suggest test plans, but let QA do and own the final ones 78 Software Engineering, Teamwork and Process issues (2) • While submitting code for review, outline test cases in separate section. This section should be sent to QA • Cover test cases you have done and test cases that need to be done by QA • Be sure to point out to possible weaknesses or issues or suspected problems in your code ➔ be open about it! 79 Software Engineering, Teamwork and Process issues (3) • Build and smoke test often, daily at the end of the cycle • Invest in automated test development (homegrown or commercial) • Number releases and keep them, so you can go back in case some bugs appear • Meet often with QA, daily at the end of the cycle Remember, QA has usually the last word on whether the product is launched 80 QA as a

I tested it via UI, works OK. I tested it using test program and APIs, and it does not work reliably. 86 Tricky bug examples (answers on possible cause) 1. Resource leaks? (memory, handles). Use Purify from IBM/Rational. Check task manager for memory usage 2. Is everything initialized? Inspect the code. Arrays are not necessarily set to 0 by the compiler. 3. What is OS doing? Microsoft roaming feature? Is OS doing some of its own “hidden” work via networking? (You would be surprised…) 4. Possible race conditions. Inspect the code for problem spots. Linearize the code, put semaphores and barriers and rerun. Use stress testing.

chunk_id: SFSU CSC648-848 [02] Fall 2025 C14 Software Engineering Review M5 and Prep Final Exam (s)-0
text: Software Engineering CSC648/848 Class Plan • Software Engineering Class Review. • M5 Q&A • Demo & Final Exam Preparation. • 1:1 Meeting with Team Lead. I will spend 5min with each team lead to review final team collaboration report. • Team Collaboration Henry VILLAR SE REVIEW Preparation for Milestone 5 and FINAL Exam 4 What are the four important attributes that all professional software should have? Four important attributes are: 1. Maintainability, 2. Dependability, 3. Performance and 4. Usability. Review Time Question Answer What is software? Computer programs and associated documentation. Software products may be developed for a particular customer or may be developed for a general market. What are the attributes of good software? Good software should deliver the required functionality and performance to the user and should be maintainable, dependable and usable. What is software engineering? Software engineering is an engineering discipline that is concerned with all aspects of software production. What are the fundamental software engineering activities? Software specification, software development, software validation and software evolution. What is the difference between software engineering and computer science? Computer science focuses on theory and fundamentals; software engineering is concerned with the practicalities of developing and delivering useful software. What is the difference between software engineering and system engineering? System engineering is concerned with all aspects of computer- based systems development including hardware, software and process engineering. Software engineering is part of this more general process. Methodologies Waterfall Evolutionary Iterative Incremental Open Source Development Extreme Programing User Centered Design (UCD) Continuous Development AGILE & SCRUM GitHub M5 Latest Code Revised Docs in M5 folder Original Docs Presentations Best Practices • Content • Structure • Packaging • Human Element • The Voice • The Body http://www.nwlink.com/~donclark/leader/leadpres.html#intro https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_Mm_q0X-R1c Henry VILLAR SOFTWARE ENGINEERING REVIEW FINAL Exam Prep Practical recommendations applying SE to real life • Critical to know what users and management expect from the product, what are the resources and deadlines ➔ high-level specs, requirements, plan and priorities are important to be developed and agreed upon • Be ready to change and evolve req. and specs, with proper approvals • For overall SE process and project organization apply SCRUM with periodic reviews review, or XP for smaller systems and demos • For user intensive subsystems and APIs and docs apply UCD • Budget adequate QA, usability testing, documentation • Take care of legalese, permissions, licensing, copyright early • Pay attention to getting/creating the best team, and maintain high team spirit and motivation as a developer or manager. Provide coffee and food and take care of the teamwork • Have a team lead who helps in technical decisions • Assess and resolve risks early (technical, resource, skills, legal, schedule..) • Establish coding style and basic documentation standards or adopt the mandated ones • Establish collaborative and SE development tools or adopt the mandated ones • Use frequent build and test • Use coding reviews • Ensure code is documented for easy maintenance • Think of the user all the time Thank You

"Software Engineering 2004": Curriculum Guidelines for Undergraduate Degree Programs in Software Engineering, The joint Task Force on Computing Curricula, IEEE Computer Society, Association for Computing Machinery, August 2004 Motivation • Most of the failures of delivering SW to specs, on time and budget, and to a user “delight” were rooted in a misunderstanding user needs, and/or poor design, planning and organization. Charette, R. N., “Why Software Fails”, IEEE Spectrum, September 2005, pp. 42 Motivation • ACM study on SW outsourcing and globalization specifically mentions the need to train SE students in so called “soft skills”, teamwork, communication, SE process organization and issues related to globalization Aspray W., Mayadas F., Vardi M.Y., Editors: “Globalization and Offshoring of Software, A Report of the ACM Job Migration Task Force”, ACM 2006, http://www.acm.org/globalizationreport/ Motivation • Check job postings for SW engineers – most require (besides specific technical skills): • Ability to work in a team • Oral and written communication • Knowledge of SE tools • Familiarity with modern SE processes and lifecycle (NEW emphasis on continuous and collaborative SE) • Awareness about user experience • Numerous student testimonials how the class helped them find the job or in current job Some things never change… SW Technology (tools, frameworks, languages, components, protocols) do change very often (yearly…) BUT Principles and best practices of SE management, organization and people stuff change little with time Hence, learning SE principles (from this class) offers you long term skills which grow in importance as you get more experienced Hard (technical) and soft (interpersonal/professional) skills for SE • Hard skills – traditional CS curriculum • Data structures, Math • Programming • Computer architectures • Database/networking • …. • BUT ALSO: Soft skills • Teamwork and communications • Ability to follow SE best practices and processes • Dealing with customers, stakeholders, management • SW development using team tools What makes great SW Engineer and how to teach SW Engineering Check ACM slides posted on Canvas Science vs. Engineering • Grow and expand knowledge • “Build in order to learn” • “Vertical” education – go into depth and details • Emphasis on individual competency • Apply knowledge to build useful products • “Learn in order to build” • Demo and folder with documentation – M5 folder. • Team grade has SE Product component (how good is the product the team developed) and SE Process (how well the team followed SE best practices) • All team members get same grade unless exceptions (low attendance, low participation etc) • Team leads may get up to 5 points extra credit for job well done • Front end and back end team leads and github master my get up to 3 points extra credit for job well done • Final exam: closed book, closed notes, in class/virtual. 40/100 of the grade (40 questions) • No homework; No midterm BUT a lot of teamwork and 6 delivery milestones (M0-M5) Canvas https://sfsu.instructure.com Class syllabus • Check Canvas Syllabus Break 10 Minutes Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC) / SE Process Lifecycle 1. Planning 2. Analysis 3. Design 4. Implementation 5. Testing & Integration 6. Maintenance SW Engineering Process Components 1. Requirements and Specification 2. Design and Implementation 3. Validation 4. Maintenance and Evolution SW Engineering Process Components • There are several basic components present in all SW Engineering methodologies: 1. SW Requirements and Specification: functionality, constraints, look and feel, GUI, performance 2. SW Design and Implementation 3. SW Validation: formal, test plans, QA 4. SW Maintenance and Evolution: ongoing changes (and fixes) to meet customer needs These components are applied in different way and order (linear, iterative, spiral...) depending on chosen SW Engineering methodology or models (I.e. waterfall, extreme, agile etc.) Important Methodologies (Process Models) Waterfall Evolutionary Iterative Incremental Open Source Development Extreme Programing User Centered Design (UCD) Continuous Development AGILE & SCRUM SW Process Models (or Methodologies) • Abstract representations of overall development of SW - methods and practices for organizing the SE effort • Waterfall • Evolutionary • Iterative • Open Source development • Extreme programming • For GUI and usability: User Centered Design (UCD) – variation of iterative development • Continuous development • Agile, Scrum – high level project management (PM) processes used to “manage, organize and implement ” the above SE processes Important Milestone 0 – individual milestone – Choose and learn team project SW tools - 10/100 • First, students have to chose the tools and get approval from Instructor/CTO • Then, they have to learn how to use chosen tools and create team page (ABOUT page) • 10/100 of the grade -Details be presented and posted on Canvas – graded for individual team members separately • Students within the team encouraged to help each other!

The email must have the format indicated in the M0 document. The due date is tonight by 11:59PM.” M0 Expectation All projects will be inspected and graded on the due date. Individual video demonstrating config-connection Today’s Topics • Personas • Use Cases • Requirements • Specifications • Priorities • High-Level Mock-up, Storyboards Class Team Project: AI LifeOS – Intelligent Student Co-Pilot Platform The system will allow students to create a personal dashboard where they can manage goals, projects, learning activities, and personal development areas such as academic success, career preparation, leadership, wellness, and life skills. A core component of the platform will be a powerful search and discovery engine that enables users to find and explore: • Goals and project templates • Productivity and study workflows • Academic and career resources • AI tools and learning aids • Campus-related opportunities and services • Public community-shared content Users will be able to search by keywords and filter results by multiple criteria such as: • Category (academic, career, wellness, leadership, finance, creativity, research, etc.) • Use case (studying, job preparation, mental wellness, skill building, productivity, project management) • Content type (tools, templates, workflows, reflections, resources, events) • Skill area (technical, soft skills, leadership, research, health, communication) • AI-enabled vs. non-AI resources • Free vs. paid (informational only, no transactions) The system will provide interactive dashboards, visual timelines, charts, and personalized reports. • The platform will support community-oriented capabilities, such as: • Browsing and searching public goal templates and workflows • Sharing resources, tools, and strategies • Discovering popular or trending student tools • Rating, tagging, and bookmarking content • In-site messaging between users (webmail only) The platform targets three primary audiences: • College Students – Primary users managing academics, career preparation, and personal development. • Faculty & Academic Programs – Secondary users interested in ethical AI use, learning support, and student development tools. • University Support Organizations – Career centers, tutoring centers, and student success programs that may showcase categorized resources. SW Maintenance and Evolution: ongoing changes (and fixes) to meet customer needs These components are applied in different way and order (linear, iterative, spiral...) depending on chosen SW Engineering methodology or models (I.e. waterfall, extreme, agile etc.) Important Use Cases, Requirements, Specifications • Personas and Use Cases: describe who are the main actors (“personas”) and then a story (“use cases”) of WHAT services/functions users will need, in plain English. Focus on WHAT not HOW. Involve marketing, sales, competitive study, focus groups • Requirements: developed from use cases, usually by marketing, engineering, sales (focus on WHAT not how): • Functional (what functions the SW has, what does it do) • Non-functional – what properties the SW has (security, privacy, performance, deploy in Cloud? etc.) • Specifications – more concrete engineering guidance of HOW to develop the SW (often combined with requirements into SW requirement specifications - SRS) • Followed by architectural design, implementation, testing in an iterative fashion 13 Important Iterations!!!!! Personas – a way to represent user types for your use cases and UI design – and testing • From Interaction Design Organization, downloaded 05/28/18 • https://www.interaction-design.org/literature/article/personas-why-and-how- you-should-use-them • Personas are fictional characters, which you create based upon your research in order to represent the different user types that might use your service, product, site, or brand in a similar way. Creating personas will help you to understand your users’ needs, experiences, behaviors and goals. Creating personas can help you step out of yourself. It can help you to recognize that different people have different needs and expectations, and it can also help you to identify with the user you’re designing for. Personas make the design task at hand less complex, they guide your ideation processes, and they can help you to achieve the goal of creating a good user experience for your target user group. 14 Personas • Personas have: • Attitude • Skills • Limitations • Pain points • Goals • Personas go over scenarios (use cases) using your application in the appropriate context (home, on the road…) • Create one page persona description (with image) to be handy during the design – some examples below • https://www.google.com/search?q=ui+design+persona&hl=en&source=lnms&tbm=isch&sa =X&ved=0ahUKEwjmu_Wv6KjbAhUfIDQIHTHTD2YQ_AUICigB&biw=1440&bih=782 15 Important Example persona: SFSU students •About [any student above, let’s call her Mary from the CS department]: •Very busy (school + perhaps a side job) •Passionate for new tech trends •Loves community/sports and technology •Environmentally conscious •Participate in justice, diversity and equality activities and events •Has advanced WWW skills •Goals and scenario: •Current job is not related to technology and want to start entering her professional field •No professional network, limited knowledge of the companies in the Bay Area, and overwhelmed from the Jobs

Must do! – Define actors in the application – persona – a model of a typical user e.g. define user skill level, pain points…. – Describe user tasks and errors – Know frequency and importance of tasks – Describe user environment and context – Describe or use data items form data glossary – be consistent • Methods for gathering information: – Ask users (single or team) – Observe users in their actual environment – Look at user documentation or any other information – Surveys, focus groups – Ask your own marketing, sales, support, client reps – Read WWW, product reviews – Ask your friends Important 20 Use Cases >> Task analysis • Task name, description and current duration as well as frequency of usage • User skill level, experience and role • Work steps including usage of other systems (printers, faxes…) • System features, GUI screens currently used • Difficulties and errors in using current system • Current systems performance and reliability • Current user satisfaction and “pain points” • Follow-up questions • Sample inputs, screens etc. • Good to compare current and future (with your system) use cases in terms of number of steps, time to complete etc. Important 21 Use cases levels of detail • High level – focus on WHAT the user does at high level (no details of HOW) • Lower-level – more details, spell out functions, steps etc. (less used) • High-level use cases are critical • Drive them by user needs NOT the constrains of the envisioned system. Compromise only if you must, be a user advocate. • Focus on WHAT user needs not HOW to design or implement Important Usage of use cases • Communicates vision of the system easily understood to non- technical people (CEO, marketing, sales, investors) • Drives the design and requirements • Use to test all aspects of the system, especially the UI • Use to develop QA plans 22 Important Examples of bad high level use case, then a corrected one • Mary wants to search for an entry tech job. She chooses the appropriate function from our www site, and only gets one result. She is prompted to register or log in to see more results • This way you ensure lazy registration implementation metaphor where user is prompted to register/login only when already using the function – this is the right way • https://mockitt.wondershare.com/ui-ux-design/ui-design-patterns.html 24 Use Cases, Requirements, Specifications • Personas and Use Cases: describe who are the main actors (“personas”) and then a story (“use cases”) of WHAT services/functions users will need, in plain English. Focus on WHT not HOW. Involve marketing, sales, competitive study, focus groups • Requirements: developed from use cases, usually by marketing, engineering, sales (focus on WHAT not how): • Functional (what functions the SW has, what does it do) • Non-functional – what properties the SW has (security, privacy, performance, deploy in Cloud? etc.) • Specifications – more concrete engineering guidance of HOW to develop the SW (often combined with requirements into SW requirement specifications - SRS) • Followed by architectural design, implementation, testing in an iterative fashion 25 Important Iterations!!!!! 26 Why Requirements? 27 Software Requirements • Descriptions of a SW system – starting point for specifications, design and implementation • One of the main and early component in any SE process – focus is on WHAT not HOW • Main vehicle of communication between: – Idea initiator and the rest of the world – Clients and SW providers – Customer and product development (SW, marketing, sales) – Product development and management – Product development and marketing – Between developers (especially at different locations) – Developers and QA • Good overview http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Requirements_analysis Important 28 What are Requirements • Requirements: Abstract statements or descriptions of SW services, functions or constraints • For high-level requirements focus is on WHAT not HOW • Level of abstraction goes from high (usually called requirement definitions) to more detailed (usually called requirement specifications) Important 29 Format and types of Requirements • Implicit, not written, “in your head only” - usually SW done for your own needs (e.g. your homework; self- education; fun) • Verbal understanding and communication (extreme programming, small team, early prototype, team project at school) • A few pages, free format (startups, small company, school) • Structured documents (commercial,

text: on WHAT not HOW • Level of abstraction goes from high (usually called requirement definitions) to more detailed (usually called requirement specifications) Important 29 Format and types of Requirements • Implicit, not written, “in your head only” - usually SW done for your own needs (e.g. your homework; self- education; fun) • Verbal understanding and communication (extreme programming, small team, early prototype, team project at school) • A few pages, free format (startups, small company, school) • Structured documents (commercial, format often fixed and specific to a company) • Very formal and elaborate (commercial, medical, avionics, government) • Essential to ensure all have the same understanding of what needs to be done • And, they change in time!!!! Formalism 30 Req. Categories: Functional and Non-functional Requirements • Functional requirements – WHAT DOES IT DO? Services and functions system provides. How will system react and behave depending on inputs. Specific functions provided to the user. Key data items • Non-Functional requirements – HOW DOES IT DELIVER? Various constrains on timing, cost, ease of use, standards, security, performance, capacity, HW/SW environment, privacy etc. • In some cases: Domain requirements (in case of certain app domains) – Comes from application domain and reflect specifics of that domain – Key in mutidisciplinary projects e.g. bioinformatics Important 31 Functional and Non-functional Requirements Functional requirements: • Functions: Describe functions and services that system provides • Also: Data description: describe key data items and entities in the system (non compulsory but extremely useful) Non-functional requirements: • System requirements: describe the system requirements (architecture, system services, networks, platforms etc.) • Usability requirements: describe specific usability and UI issues, users who will use the system, delivery clients • Performance requirements: describe system performance (speed, accuracy, latency, delay, bandwidth etc.) • Storage, security, environmental requirements • Marketing, legal requirements (logos, branding, licensing) • Content (size, formats…) • Privacy (what data is collected, how is it used…) Important 32 Functional Requirements 1) Functions: Describe functions and services provided by the system • Examples: – Users shall be able to search using image categories – Users shall be able to purchase music files using credit card – Music shall be accessible by author and title Note: may contains some usability requirements (in choices of functions available), but usability can be addressed also in non-functional requirements too) Use SHALL Not: will, may, should… Important 33 Functional requirements 2) Data description: Describe key entities and data items and give them unique and meaningful names – Main actors/users in the system (admin, customer…) and their roles – Key data elements (book item, shopping cart, registration, search metadata). Important to describe digital content (see next slide) – For each have the name, sub-items, means of input/initialization, usage, storage etc. • Use these names consistently for documentation, UI, reference, naming of classes and variables, database schema • Proper data description is the key for communication and understanding between/among developers and clients Important 34 Examples of High-level Requirements • System shall provide full functionality without using mouse • System shall provide user access to summary as well as raw data • User shall be able to edit and correct the data after it is submitted • System shall provide access to all data items based on customer name and account number • Customer transactions shall be executed via

text: Proper data description is the key for communication and understanding between/among developers and clients Important 34 Examples of High-level Requirements • System shall provide full functionality without using mouse • System shall provide user access to summary as well as raw data • User shall be able to edit and correct the data after it is submitted • System shall provide access to all data items based on customer name and account number • Customer transactions shall be executed via secure internet connection • Registration form: required for users to register. Contains name and e-mail, and optionally address, phone, affiliation. Stored in the database. • Privacy: Data is used only by this WWW site for tracking the customer usage and shall not be shared with anybody else. Registered users may be contacted withy questions and promotions. Functional requirements are prioritized • Details will be covered later but the key is to prioritize functions • Helps project planning • Helps UI design – high priority functions deserve more prominent UI exposure 35 Important 36 Non-Functional Requirements • Refer to system properties and constraints such as: – Reliability – Response time – HW and networking requirements – Usability requirements – Marketing, legal, licensing – Media content (formats, size…) – Privacy: what is the data collected, how is the data used – Compatibility (e.g. which browsers…) – Can refer to: – Product (product behavior like speed, reliability) – Organization (e.g. process, standards used) – External factors (e.g. branding, legal disclaimers displayed) As important as functional requirements. Important 37 Non-Functional Requirements (2) • Examples: – System shall response visually within 5 seconds – File size in no time shall exceed 2 Mbytes – Users with high-school diploma, after 1 hour training, shall complete the task in 5 minutes with no more than 2 errors. – Tools from XYZ shall be used for requirement management – Each requirement shall have identifiable portion of code associated with it referenced by module name and code line number – Each WWW page shall have official company logo in upper left corner – The following user data shall be colleted…; the data shall be used ONLY for for …. Non-functional requirements (3) • Determined often by nontechnical constraints: • Business needs • Legal, marketing, copyright • Not prioritized – they are all required • Can not be changed solely by eng. team - change requires all stakeholders 38 Important 39 Another classification of requirement categories – High-level requirements or vision or user requirements: unstructured, natural language with some diagrams – System requirements or more detailed requirements: more structured description of system functions and services. Can serve as a contract between client and developer. Natural language, structured language, simple mockups, data flow diagrams, charts – Software specifications: more detailed SW description to serve as basis for design. Used by developers. Natural language, block diagrams, formulae, program design language (PDL), flow charts, data flow diagrams, mockups, structured language, database schemas etc. (Exact formats and naming conventions often dictated by company policies) Low detail High level of detail Important 40 Requirements: who reads them • User or High-level Requirements: clients, users, client engineers, contract managers, systems architects, legal, marketing, executives • System requirements: users, client engineers, system architects, SW developers • Software Design Specs: System architects, SW developers, maybe client SW developers,

Write from “outside-in” perspective • Leave design decisions (HOW) for later • Use SHALL for must do, SHOULD for optional • One concept per requirement • Cover all necessary issues • Avoid jargon • Number them for tracking • Group by priorities then by actors/subsystyems • Talk in positive logic • Details added as we go to lower-level requirements • Not appropriate high level requirements: – Function X shall be provided by two buttons in upper left corner, and system should be fast. – Why? Important 42 WHAT vs. HOW • WHAT: what do you want, what the users want, what is the ultimate goal…➔ used for high level requirements, specs • HOW: how to do this, how to design this ➔ for lower level, more detailed specs, design, implementation • Ideally, first focus on WHAT then on HOW • WHY? • What happens if you do HOW too early? Important 43 Requirement measures • As much as possible, and where it applies, put objectively measurable “tests” to verify that the requirement is fulfilled – “System should be easy to use” is OK as high level requirement from CEO, but without more details does not help design and QA. • Need to add more details at next level, e.g.: – 80% of novice users to complete the process in 5 minutes with no more than 2 errors after 1 hour of training Still WHAT and not HOW 44 Requirements measures (2) Property Measure Speed Processed transactions/second User/Event response time Screen refresh time Size K Bytes Number of RAM chips Ease of use Training time Number of help frames Reliability Mean time to failure Probability of unavailability Rate of failure occurrence Availability Robustness Time to restart after failure Percentage of events causing failure Probability of data corruption on failure Portability Percentage of target dependent statements Number of target systems From: Ian Sommerville Use Cases, Requirements, Specifications • Personas and Use Cases: describe who are the main actors (“personas”) and then a story (“use cases”) of WHAT services/functions users will need, in plain English. Requirements vs. Specifications • Distinction is blurred • One way of thinking: requirements focus on WHAT and specifications add more of HOW information • Often merged into Software Requirements Specification documents (SRS) • http://techwhirl.com/skills/tech-docs/writing-software-requirements-specs/ • SW specs: more concrete design and programming guidance (program design language, block diagrams, choice of platforms, compilers, etc,) 46 47 Typical Content of a General Requirement • Title • Reference number (for tracking) • Text (a few lines or a few paragraphs) • Graphics, diagrams (optional), mandatory for UI • Initiator, owner • Priority e.g. 1, 2, 3 (can be assigned early or later) Many companies have their own templates (government, military, IEEE, ACM) Follow the required process (or work with management to improve it) Important 48 RTM (Requirements Traceability Matrix) VMware Keep the ID until the end Requirement / Use case. Brief. Functional or Technical, and description 49 Typical Content of a General Requirement - Text • Description of service, function or constraints to be achieved • Use case (or reference to it) • Error conditions, special cases • Measures to verify • For data glossary: name, description, usage, behavior, storage Important 50 Product Requirement Documents • Requirements are collected in a document. Early requirements are often called Marketing or Product Requirement Document (MRD or PRD). Such documents are official (approved, tracked, dates etc.). • Use consistent format throughout the project • Companies often have their own internal formats • Use language in a consistent way. “Shall” means mandatory, “should” means optional. • Avoid usage of jargon • Data dictionary/Glossary at the beginning helps • Keep change history: date, contributor’s name, summary of change 1-3 lines • https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Product_requirements_document 51 Requirements document (PRD/MRD) management • Can be made arbitrarily complex, to the point that it becomes goal in itself! Be aware! • Make is simple to enter, modify and track • Use established tools and standards (MS Word, Excel, HTML) • Be careful if you want to use special tools. Problems are: cost, training, wide availability, vendor lock in • Important to keep the versions/dates • Again: sometimes this will be imposed on you by the company, client etc. Competitive analysis • In design of any system one needs to know • Goals and objectives • Competitive landscape – who is doing something similar • Relationship of your planned product with current market leaders • Developed as competitive analysis (jointly with marketing) • All CEOs, managers and investors ask this up front 52 Important 53 Competitive analysis– know the background; who did what; and how your envision product compares • Feature and performance comparison with competitors • Try competitor products yourself and compare (be hones) • Survey product review literature and analyze gaps • Know industry trends and leverage them • Use GUI metaphors established in relevant industry area • Define priorities from competitive standpoint (I.e. reviewers/customers always point that competitors have
