++ superior; - does not exist Important Highlight your product Requirements solicitation – gathering requirements • Requirement Solicitation/elicitation: Process of gathering requirements • Issues • Scope – boundaries of the system to be built • Understanding – customers do not understand it either • Changes – requirements change in time (better undemanding, changing business/competitive environments, costs, schedules) • Cost effective methods of gathering – interviews, meetings, focus groups, marketing research…. http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Requirements_elicitation Summerville and Sawyer, 1997 Important 55 Steps in req. solicitation • Define requirement gathering methods (focus groups, meetings, interviews…). • Identify key stakeholders and users and involve them • Define technical environment for the system (architecture, OS, connections….) • Business and marketing analysis • Use case analysis • Develop requirements: • Uses cases to functional specs and data glossary • Constrains and business/marketing issues to nonfunctional specs • Document in Marketing Requirement Document (MRD) • Communicate, revise, manage MRD Important REQUIREMENTS CHANGE SO REVISE THEM AS NEEDED 56 57 Issues and problems with requirements for SW • Full requirement set is almost never known at early stage • Requirements most often change in the process due to real reasons (people learned more, business changed, users requested it after evaluation) and some other reasons (marketing wants more, competition is there, miscommunication) ➔ recall why it is hard to manage SW projects…. • We will address this in the class (e.g. this is why Waterfall method does not work) • This is one of the key problems in building and managing large SW systems Important 58 Issues and problems with requirements (2) • Ambiguity: can be interpreted (and will be interpreted) differently by different people or groups. • Incompleteness: do not specify all what needs to be specified. (Authors assumed that others know too much) • Conflicting • Hard to verify • Hard to use formats and document management • It is very hard to create perfect requirements. Don’t try to be perfect, be good enough for product to succeed! • Requirements are only means to an end (I.e. successful product) Important 59 Waterfall and other models that required all req. to be known ahead of the time: some history • David Parnas, Paul Clements, 1986: – A system’s users seldom know exactly what they want and cannot articulate all they want – Even if we could state all the requirements, there are many details that we can only discover once we are well into implementation – Even if we knew all these details, as humans, we can master only

text: product) Important 59 Waterfall and other models that required all req. to be known ahead of the time: some history • David Parnas, Paul Clements, 1986: – A system’s users seldom know exactly what they want and cannot articulate all they want – Even if we could state all the requirements, there are many details that we can only discover once we are well into implementation – Even if we knew all these details, as humans, we can master only so much complexity – Even if we could master all this complexity, external forces lead to changes in requirements, some of which may invalidate earlier decisions Important 60 Priorities • It is critical to have priorities attached to each requirement so you can make choices when schedules and costs and resources get constrained • Agree on priorities with marketing, executives and customers • It never happens that all features are equally important – Priority 1: Critical: do not launch product without it. – Priority 2: Important: it would be really good to have it. Don’t delay ship date if not available – Priority 3: Opportunistic: nice to have. Candidate for next release. • When it comes to cutting functions/SCOPE (always happens late in the schedule) use priorities to make a decision • Only used for functions to be provided, not for data glossary or non-functional requirements Important Priorities vary from domain to domain • Medical: patient and staff safety; accuracy; privacy; ease of use • R&D, Academic: accuracy; performance; ease of use • Banking: transaction accuracy; security; reliability; availability • Games: graphics, story, usability • Check this early and use to guide design and project management/resource optimization 61 Priorities and specs • Functional specs • Critical to prioritize • Priorities used to guide the design and decisions what to keep/drop/add • Helps plan the development (focus on priority 1 first) • Grouping by priorities (especially Priority 1) helps better understand what the product features are • Nonfunctional specs • Usually fixed and given by funding client/management/legal/marketing • Generally not prioritized – all must be done • Can not be changed solely by engineering team 62 Important 63 Must Have Critical to the current delivery in order for it to be a success. If one Must have is not included, the project delivery should be considered a failure Should Have Important but not necessary for delivery in the current stage. While Should haves can be as important as Must haves, they are often not as time-critical Could Have Desirable but not necessary and could improve user experience or customer satisfaction for little development cost. Included if time and resources permit. Won’t Have The least-critical, lowest-payback items, or not appropriate at that time agreed as out of scope MoSCoW Priority (VMware) 64 Requirements for UI and User-Intensive Systems • Similar rules apply PLUS: – Strongly driven by use cases and types/categories of users - personas – Considerations related to usability, inherent characteristics of intended users and UI design principles – It is very hard to communicate and specify such requirements only with text – users need to see it before they can understand or decide ➔ use of graphical mockups and storyboards is essential (with some text please) Important High Level Use Cases for User Intensive Systems • Starting point. • Covered

text: PLUS: – Strongly driven by use cases and types/categories of users - personas – Considerations related to usability, inherent characteristics of intended users and UI design principles – It is very hard to communicate and specify such requirements only with text – users need to see it before they can understand or decide ➔ use of graphical mockups and storyboards is essential (with some text please) Important High Level Use Cases for User Intensive Systems • Starting point. • Covered before – also: important to defer specific UI decisions to later – focus on WHAT users want to do and not HOW (HOW will be done later – mockups, prototypes, feedback…) • And also • Summarize typical users by set of fictitious “personas” with their characteristics important for the application 65 Important 66 From Use Cases and Personas to High-level Mockups, storyboards • Simplified graphical representations of envisioned UI • Focus is on UI basics: functions and behaviors, layout, flow • Graphics none or very minimal so as to not grab the attention • High level mockups are best B&W! (Color distracts from UX analysis) • Text description for all functions and main issues • Format: wire diagrams, HTML, hand drawings, animation • Mandatory for UI requirements and specifications Important 67 VMlist VMware Toolbar Status (configuration, setup) or MKS (Mouse, screen, Keyboard area) Status messages: mouse release, tools install etc. UI Mockups – design of VMWare Workstation 3.0 (using “wire diagrams”) 68 Storyboards – sequence of mockups following a use case Use case 1 Use case 2 Important 69 Why are simple wire diagrams best in early stage? • Easy to produce (can be hand drawn too) • Easy to change (no coding invested) • Allow easy scribbling, markup • No graphics or colors help focus on key issues: function and layout • Allow considerable “scenario and usage” testing • (Many great designs done on a napkin…) Important UI Mockups and storyboards – resources (templates, examples) • http://webdesignledger.com/inspiration/18-great-examples-of-sketched-ui- wireframes-and-mockups • https://www.visual-paradigm.com/guide/ux-design/wireframe-vs-storyboard- vs-wireflow-vs-mockup-vs-prototyping/ • And there are tools for this • Review – top 21 • https://blog.prototypr.io/top-20-prototyping-tools-for-ui-and-ux-designers-2017-46d59be0b3a9 • Figma https://www.figma.com/ • Sketch https://www.sketch.com/ 70 71 Requirements for Media Rich apps (almost all today) • Raw data: Files used to render content (MPEG, PDF, JPEG etc.) • Metadata: data describing content, used for searching • Supporting data: data used to display items in the search result list. In the form of text, but also may include key frames, samples, previews • Price • Copyright rules Used to design both search as well as database organization Requires close interaction with data owners, librarians etc. Important 72 How to develop requirements regarding content organization • This is information/data design issue not a coding issue • Interview current owners and users • Base it on search needs of users as well as nature of the content (common ways of describing content) • Look at similar other products or applications • Review legacy (current usage) • Follow legal rules on content usage • Application dependent: • Movies • Stock photo • Books • Shopping Important 73 Story about requirements – some wisdom and experience • Requirements are very important, BUT be aware of perils: • Insisting on complete requirements is most often an obstacle to SW development. They simply may not be known at that point

They simply may not be known at that point • Same holds for excessive, inflexible and cumbersome documentation and requirement process mechanics • “Requirement paralysis” ➔ endless discussions, while schedules are being missed • Break the impasse by iterative method and early prototyping, then refine • There are applications where requirements are critical (mission critical, safety, medical, military, legal) • Use judgment (as always) as to how much to invest in requirements process Thank You

chunk_id: SFSU CSC648-848 [02] Spring 2026 C3 Arch Intro - SE Teamwork-0
text: Software Engineering CSC648/848 Henry VILLAR ARCHITECTURE INTRO & SE TEAMWORK Intro to Architecture Design Pattern & Teamwork vs. group work, Week 2 Summary 1. What are Personas? 2. What are Use Cases? 3. What are Requirements? 4. What are Specifications? 5. What are Priorities, how do you prioritize? 6. Mockups and Storyboards? Summary. Cont. 1. What are Personas? Character that represents the different user types that might use your applications (service, product, site, or brand in a similar way) 2. What are Use Cases? Services and functions personas will use your envisioned system 3. What are Requirements? Abstract statements or descriptions of SW services, functions or constraints focus on WHAT • Functional (what functions the SW has, what does it do) • Non-functional – what properties the SW has (security, privacy, performance, deploy in Cloud? etc.) 4. What are Specifications? More concrete engineering guidance of HOW to develop the SW 5. What are Priorities, how do you prioritize? It is critical to have priorities attached to each requirement so you can make choices when schedules and costs and resources get constrained. P1 Critical (must have), P2 Important (should/Could have), P3 Opportunistic (Nice to have) 6. What are Mockups and Storyboards? Simplified graphical representations of envisioned UI to guide the implementation and the development understanding Data Items and Entities (Entity Relationship Diagram – ERD) Example requirements for the insulin pump software system Source: Medical Economics https://www.medicaleconomics.com/view/certain-insulin-pumps-risk-hacking The structured specification of a requirement for an insulin pump Use cases for the Mentcare system Chapter 4 Requirements Engineering 8 W2 Applicability – Class Exercise • SCENARIO Your start-up is assigned to develop a web application for project participation metrics reporting service for instructors as a collaborative platform for teaching, training and managing teamwork in academia or industry. This is very important application for educators and software managers since teamwork during software development is increasingly becoming one of the key skills required for students to be competitive in a global development environment. Users of the system are instructors teaching collaborative project development, TA, Software Managers, Product Managers, Students and Developers Engineers. Some instructors‘ level of computing knowledge should be considered low but they are familiar with using basic Web and office applications (word processing etc.). They do not necessarily understand internals of Web and database applications. They will not be trained in using this system; hence they expect considerable ease of use and an intuitive interface. But most of the other users are computer savvy. The app needs to collect the following data: Version Repository, Bug Tracking, Mailing List, Discussion Forum, Documentation among other. The app needs to display the following information as minimum: Tabular and Graphical Data comparing all current projects Users shall have to register and then login in order to use the system System shall respond to user query in less than 3 seconds. On-line help shall be provided Cont. • ASK • PENDING. Executive Summary 1. Personas & Use Cases 2. Data items and entities 3. Functional Requirements 4. Non-functional requirements 5. Mockup and Storyboard 6. Competitive Analysis • PENDING. High-Level system architecture • Team & Roles W2 Applicability – Class Exercise • SCENARIO Your start-up is assigned to develop a web application for project participation metrics reporting service for instructors as a collaborative platform for teaching, training and managing

text: Apple Watch Application for an AI tool interacting with any existing Health application • Front End Design (UI) • Architecture Design Pattern • Duration: 5 min • Deliverables (via text, block diagrams, or UML): • Apple watch Mock-up of the AI tool UI • Chosen Architecture Design Pattern SW Architecture and Design Patterns • Understand the importance and role of SW architecture design • Arch. Design patterns: usage and benefits • UML – Unified Modeling Language Architecture Design • Architectural design is concerned with understanding how a software system should be organized and designing the overall structure of that system. • Architectural design is the critical link between design and requirements engineering, as it identifies the main structural components in a system and the relationships between them. • The output of the architectural design process is an architectural model that describes how the system is organized as a set of communicating components. The software architecture of an ATM system Source: https://empireatmgroup.com/shop/gen mega-2500-atm-machine/ Source: Internet Architectural design patterns • Predefined and proven high level solutions for particular class of problems • Usage: Analyze your application requirements then try to map into one of the known architectural patterns, then evaluate to chose the best • Examples: • Model-View-Controller; • Multitier architectures. Layered Architecture (or Three (Four) Tier Arch.); • Repository; • Client-Server • http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Architectural_pattern Important 20 Some resources on architectural patterns • MVC • http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MVC_Pattern • Multitier architectures • http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/3-tier_architecture • Client server • http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Client_server • Blackboard • http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Blackboard_system 21 MVC – preferred model/pattern for the team project • Good explanation with example codes • http://www.tomdalling.com/blog/software-design/model- view-controller-explained/ • What each subsystem “needs to know” about others? (The less it knows the more modular it is…) • There are some differing definitions as to what model can do and knows about – does it also do some business logic/data interpretation? • We prefer that model simply manages data, and business logic is separate (in controller) • Why is this good? 22 The Model-View-Controller (MVC) pattern Name MVC (Model-View-Controller) Description Separates presentation and interaction from the system data. The system is structured into three logical components that interact with each other. The Model component manages the system data and associated operations on that data. The View component defines and manages how the data is presented to the user. The Controller component manages user interaction (e.g., key presses, mouse clicks, etc.) and passes these interactions to the View and the Model. See Figure 6.3. Example Figure 6.4 shows the architecture of a web-based application system organized using the MVC pattern. When used Used when there are multiple ways to view and interact with data. Also used when the future requirements for interaction and presentation of data are unknown. Advantages Allows the data to change independently of its representation and vice versa. Supports presentation of the same data in different ways with changes made in one representation shown in all of them. Disadvantages Can involve additional code and code complexity when the data model and interactions are simple. Chapter 6 Architectural Design 23 09/02/2026 Model-View-Controller (in the context of Internet) (adapted from I. Sommerwille) Advantages: Independently treats Data from presentation; Easy to change data presentation Disadvantages: Adds complexity Important 24 Model-View-Controller modification Controller HTTP requests processing, data validation Model: Data/Database View Dynamic page validation etc. Browsers

Source: https://webformdeveloping.wordpress.com/2014/04/07/mvc/ The Layered architecture pattern Name Layered architecture Description Organizes the system into layers with related functionality associated with each layer. A layer provides services to the layer above it so the lowest-level layers represent core services that are likely to be used throughout the system. See Figure 6.6. Example A layered model of a system for sharing copyright documents held in different libraries, as shown in Figure 6.7. When used Used when building new facilities on top of existing systems; when the development is spread across several teams with each team responsibility for a layer of functionality; when there is a requirement for multi-level security. Advantages Allows replacement of entire layers so long as the interface is maintained. Redundant facilities (e.g., authentication) can be provided in each layer to increase the dependability of the system. Disadvantages In practice, providing a clean separation between layers is often difficult and a high-level layer may have to interact directly with lower-level layers rather than through the layer immediately below it. Performance can be a problem because of multiple levels of interpretation of a service request as it is processed at each layer. Chapter 6 Architectural Design 26 09/02/2026 A generic layered architecture Chapter 6 Architectural Design 27 09/02/2026 Source: https://hub.packtpub.com/what-is-multi-layered-software-architecture/ Layered Architecture: example of Internet based library system (adapted from I. Sommerwille) Web browser interface Login, query manager, print manager Search, rights management, accounting DATA access Various databases Usage: Complex large systems; Built on top of Existing systems; Security; Formal DB usage Advantage Modular; Easy to interface and replace layers Disadvantage Complex; Not always easy To separate layers Variant: 3 or 4 tier WWW arch Important 28 (View) (Controller (and business logic)) (Model) The Repository pattern Name Repository Description All data in a system is managed in a central repository that is accessible to all system components. Components do not interact directly, only through the repository. Example Figure 6.9 is an example of an IDE where the components use a repository of system design information. Each software tool generates information which is then available for use by other tools. When used You should use this pattern when you have a system in which large volumes of information are generated that has to be stored for a long time. You may also use it in data-driven systems where the inclusion of data in the repository triggers an action or tool. Advantages Components can be independent—they do not need to know of the existence of other components. Changes made by one component can be propagated to all components. All data can be managed consistently (e.g., backups done at the same time) as it is all in one place. Disadvantages The repository is a single point of failure so problems in the repository affect the whole

text: in data-driven systems where the inclusion of data in the repository triggers an action or tool. Advantages Components can be independent—they do not need to know of the existence of other components. Changes made by one component can be propagated to all components. All data can be managed consistently (e.g., backups done at the same time) as it is all in one place. Disadvantages The repository is a single point of failure so problems in the repository affect the whole system. May be inefficiencies in organizing all communication through the repository. Distributing the repository across several computers may be difficult. Chapter 6 Architectural Design 29 09/02/2026 A repository architecture for an IDE Chapter 6 Architectural Design 30 09/02/2026 The Client–server pattern Name Client-server Description In a client–server architecture, the functionality of the system is organized into services, with each service delivered from a separate server. Clients are users of these services and access servers to make use of them. Example Figure 6.11 is an example of a film and video/DVD library organized as a client–server system. When used Used when data in a shared database has to be accessed from a range of locations. Because servers can be replicated, may also be used when the load on a system is variable. Advantages The principal advantage of this model is that servers can be distributed across a network. General functionality (e.g., a printing service) can be available to all clients and does not need to be implemented by all services. Disadvantages Each service is a single point of failure so susceptible to denial of service attacks or server failure. Performance may be unpredictable because it depends on the network as well as the system. May be management problems if servers are owned by different organizations. Can the best idea win? Can the team execute and deliver? Important 47 Managing conflict - some rules Always present, the key is how you manage it: 1. First try to resolve within the group among involved parties 2. If that does not work, involve team lead or trusted outside person 3. If that does not work involve management For global environment: 1. First try to resolve issue within local groups 2. If that does not work involve team leads from all groups 3. If that does not work, escalate to management from all groups 4. Ultimately, one single person resolves the conflict Important Team Project Time • Work on M1 • 7-10 pages. images for introduction, travel if possible • Create common goals • Be sensitive to cultural and diversity issues • Create process for conflict resolution Important 62 Behavior and attitude are critical in conflict management • Separate technical from personal issues • Be courteous • Focus on problems, not personalities • Fight for your ideas, but must ultimately think of what is best for Planning 2. Analysis 3. Design 4. Implementation 5. Testing & Integration 6. Maintenance SW Engineering Process Components 1. Requirements and Specification 2. Design and Implementation 3. Validation 4. Maintenance and Evolution SW Engineering Process Components • There are several basic components present in all SW Engineering methodologies: 1. SW Requirements and Specification: functionality, constraints, look and feel, GUI, performance 2. SW Design and Implementation 3. SW Validation: formal, test plans, QA 4. SW Maintenance and Evolution: ongoing changes (and fixes) to meet customer needs These components are applied in different way and order (linear, iterative, spiral...) depending on chosen SW Engineering methodology or models (I.e. waterfall, extreme, agile etc.) Methodologies Waterfall Evolutionary Iterative Incremental Open Source Development Extreme Programing User Centered Design (UCD) Continuous Development AGILE & SCRUM Requirements definition System and software design Implementation and unit testing Integration and system testing Operation and maintenance Validate increment Develop system increment Design system architecture Integrate increment Validate system Define outline requirements Assign requirements to increments System incomplete Final system Validation Final version Development Intermediate versions Specification Initial version Outline description Concurrent activities Waterfall Evolutionary Incremental Extreme Programming UCD AGILE SCRUM Continuous Thank You

Architectural design decisions Chapter 6 Architectural Design 6 Architecture and system characteristics • Performance • Localize critical operations and minimize communications. Use large rather than fine-grain components. • Security • Use a layered architecture with critical assets in the inner layers. • Safety • Localize safety-critical features in a small number of sub-systems. • Availability • Include redundant components and mechanisms for fault tolerance. • Maintainability • Use fine-grain, replaceable components. Chapter 6 Architectural Design 7 Architectural views Chapter 6 Architectural Design 8 16/02/2026 4 + 1 view model of software architecture • A logical view, which shows the key abstractions in the system as objects or object classes. • A process view, which shows how, at run-time, the system is composed of interacting processes. • A development view, which shows how the software is decomposed for development. • A physical view, which shows the system hardware and how software components are distributed across the processors in the system. • Related using use cases or scenarios (+1) Chapter 6 Architectural Design 9 16/02/2026 How to communicate/describe high level architecture • Text (list components) • Simple block diagrams (informal) – sometimes with special tools • Unified Modeling Language – UML – formal way • http://edn.embarcadero.com/article/31863 • UML Tools: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_Unified_Modeling_Language_tools Important Architectural design patterns • Predefined and proven high level solutions for particular class of problems • Usage: Analyze your application requirements then try to map into one of the known architectural patterns, then evaluate to chose the best • Examples: • Model-View-Controller; • Multitier architectures. Layered Architecture (or Three (Four) Tier Arch.); • Repository; • Client-Server • http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Architectural_pattern Important 11 Pipe and filter architecture • Functional transformations process their inputs to produce outputs. • May be referred to as a pipe and filter model (as in UNIX shell - inspired by the Unix technique of connecting the output of an application to the input of another via pipes on the shell.). • Variants of this approach are very common. When transformations are sequential, this is a batch sequential model which is extensively used in data processing systems. • Not really suitable for interactive systems. text: to produce outputs. • May be referred to as a pipe and filter model (as in UNIX shell - inspired by the Unix technique of connecting the output of an application to the input of another via pipes on the shell.). • Variants of this approach are very common. When transformations are sequential, this is a batch sequential model which is extensively used in data processing systems. • Not really suitable for interactive systems. Chapter 6 Architectural Design 12 16/02/2026 The pipe and filter pattern Name Pipe and filter Description The processing of the data in a system is organized so that each processing component (filter) is discrete and carries out one type of data transformation. The data flows (as in a pipe) from one component to another for processing. Example Figure 6.13 is an example of a pipe and filter system used for processing invoices. When used Commonly used in data processing applications (both batch- and transaction-based) where inputs are processed in separate stages to generate related outputs. Advantages Easy to understand and supports transformation reuse. Workflow style matches the structure of many business processes. Evolution by adding transformations is straightforward. Can be implemented as either a sequential or concurrent system. Disadvantages The format for data transfer has to be agreed upon between communicating transformations. Each transformation must parse its input and unparse its output to the agreed form. This increases system overhead and may mean that it is impossible to reuse functional transformations that use incompatible data structures. Systems >> Functionality >> Search • Browse by category (e.g. from the list) • Free Text entry field (possibly with domain selection pulldown) • Parametric search e.g. form-like search (can put value limits on variables like title, price, image size, video duration) – fixed structure • Advanced, Boolean (can create ad-hoc logical combinations of search conditions) • Content based retrieval – search based on automatically extracted information from raw media (give me images “like” this) - R&D topics for now simple complex Important 26 Components of search functions • Metadata – describe items in terms of text or numerical data which is used for searching. Input to search or indexing algorithm • Index - a data structure enabling search, computed off-line during indexing phase. Input: search arguments; Output: pointers to relevant items. Issue: when you add the data do you need to reindex the whole DB or not? • Search arguments (what is used to search with) and scope (what domain is searched e.g. the whole WWW, local WWW etc.) • Search algorithm – steps and functions used to do indexing (often called indexing alg.) or search. KW search, text search, SQL, NL etc. Can be done on index (which is pre-computed) or run-time ( e.g. running SQL) • Search UI: how is it offered to the user: a) entering search arguments and scope; b) viewing results; c) refining Important 27 STUDY MATERIALS Search systems performance measures • Precision: out of R retrieved elements, how many (RR) are relevant: RR/R • Recall: out of total of NR relevant items in the whole database/domain, how many are retrieved (RR) in specific query: RR/NR • (Precision is usually easiest to measure in WWW context since NR (total number of relevant items) is usually not known) • Indexing efficiency: how long it takes to index the database • Run time efficiency: how long does it take to perform search Important 29 SW Frameworks • Platforms to help develop SW applications – an “environment” including tools, APIs, compilers etc. • http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Software_framework • Offer tools and environment on top of APIs • Provide easier ways to build SW systems with the set of prebuilt major functions (tables, login, calendar etc.) and ensures for example cross browser and mobile device compatibility (Bootstrap) • Commercial and open source solutions available BUT 30 Important Questions to ask re: Frameworks • Functionality: will it do what I need to do now AND in the future • Maintainability: how do I maintain the system built by this framework • Licensing, terms of use, price • Cross platform issues • Learning curve of the team to use it • Availability of support and documentation • Market share, how widely used, stability of the company producing it, availability of skilled people Do not believe the marketing – VERIFY: install, try and test it for YOUR application and functions If you decide to use specific frameworks read documentation and follow instructions (this is recurring problem in our SE class) Use frameworks properly and do not hack around it! Important 31 Architectural design “Process of designing SW system organization” • Driven by non-functional specs (which are usually driven by business needs and cost) • Facilitates discussion about the SW design (needs to be reviewed and adopted) • Needs to be followed and enforced (or revised) • Helps in planning and cost estimates • Helps communicate the design to the team and other stakeholders • Always do high-level architecture review early in the process Important 32 Module 1 Module 4 Module 5 Module 3 Module 2 Module 6 Network Process Data (Marc) Display Data (Dragutin) Transform Data (Rainer) Database (Marc) (COSEIT) (HoneyComb) (CSSFSU) Legend: In house dev License/ Open Source High level arch: show major systems, function, owners, location Helps in: Design review Team comm. text: design pattern • How to distribute the system across multiple processors • Does the architecture support the system, data size and load requirements • How to decompose system into subsystems – MODULAR design (high intermodule cohesion, loose coupling) • Does the architecture satisfies non-functional requirements and costs • How it should be reviewed and documented • What existing farmeowrk can be used best to implement it Important 34 Questions to ask in Arch. Design process re implementation (2) • Are the chosen tools and frameworks reliable, used in the marketplace, tested and supported • Does the team know how to use them • Licensing, legalese, cost • Maintenance • Stability of the provider 35 Important How to decompose system into subsystems – system design • This is crucial to good SW design irrespective of technologies used! • Partition and group requirements and define subsystems • Specify subsystems – make it modular: • Functionality (well grouped and cohesive, focus on one category of functions) • Interfaces (ensure lose coupling, go for standard ones, well accepted in the market) Important 36 37 Goal of partitioning – modular design • Modularity: • Intra-subsystem cohesion: The subsystem has well defined and cohesive function (I.e. data gathering, not data gathering and data processing). • Test: can you describe its function in one simple sentence • High intersystem independence or loose coupling (I.e. separate data storage from data rendering) • Clean, well defined preferably standard interfaces • Benefits: • Maintainability • Easy to debug, QA • Easy to optimize for security, speed • “With modular programming, concerns are separated such that modules perform logically discrete functions, interacting through well-defined interfaces.” ref http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Modular_programming • Examples” MVC, SQL etc. Important Examples of partitioning • System/method X has function to obtain and process data • Wrong! Make two separate subsystems/methods/classes. Why? • Group all data access into one subsystem, one code. Test it well and enforce that only this code is used. • Why? Think of testing and tuning for performance or security • Use industry standard APIs (application programming interface) and communication protocols • Better use suboptimal but standard API and protocols than custom one of your own 38 Architecture design patterns: structure • Description (not the code) • Example • When used • Advantages • Disadvantages 39 Example of metadata for movie • Video format • Duration • Title • Rating • Time taken • Author • Actors • Director • Credits • Brief description • Number of scenes • Promotional images • Movie trailer (type, format, duration) • Music scores (number,type,format) • Links to press releases • Number of scenes • For each scene • Description • Actors • Duration • Keyframes • Video Metadata encoded in XML or DB tables 40 How to design metadata for media search • This is more like a librarian and information organization effort and NOT a coding job • Think of: • What users want to search for • Characteristics of the media content (movies, images, music) commonly used to search for it • Avoid too many search fields which are ANDed – most often produces empty result • Pull-down preferred from free text typing – avoids typos and also refes to existing content 41 Raw media Meta data Convert to Schema (DB, XML) Store (files, DB) Index the

Systems Architectures – historical evolution • Mostly client server or layered arch. with varying degree of data integration in the back-end • Media in standalone on files, CDRom: collection of interlinked hard wired files; Traditional DB with metadata in DB tables and pointers to raw medias on file systems • BLOBs: Binary Large Objects – integrates media files into the DB • https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Binary_large_object • https://dev.mysql.com/doc/refman/5.7/en/blob.html • Media content management– full applications buiolt on top of the above • Trade-offs: cost, complexity, administration, performance, security, recovery, manageability etc. Important 44 ID Autho r Title Image Link to media Item1 Miles Davis Something Else Album cover image Pointer to MP3 Music file Item2 Rolling stones Satisfaction Album cover image Pointer to MP3 music file Table in relational DB MP3 repository Application Server Clients MM Systems With DB and raw media In separate files Images can be stored in DB tables or externally Important 45 ID Autho r Title Image BLOB Item1 Miles Davis Something Else Album cover image raw MP3 Music file Item2 Rolling stones Satisfaction Album cover image Raw MP3 music file Table in relational DB Application Server Clients MM Systems With DB and BLOBS Important 46 MM Inf. Systems with DB and raw media in separate file system • Pros: • Simple • Efficient • Existing media data kept in place • Media data in their natural format • Easy to load and access media data –no DB overhead • Cons: • No admin and access control • Hard to manage access by many users • No transaction security (backup, roll-back etc.) • Data on files easily gets out of synch with metadata in DB • No leveraging of all the DB tools and functionality such as distribution etc. • Files vs. BLOBs is complex and controversial (efficiency, overhead, also deepens on file size) Important 47 BLOBs may Help here Key problem in use of classic relational DB for metadata management • Metadata vary in format and change in time • Often have messing elements • Hard to fix it ahead of time (which is required for Relational DB implementation) • Too brittle and inflexible • Slow in data ingest/input • Hard to use in real applications • BUT….NO-SQL to the rescue 48 NEW: No-SQL for managing metadata • Driven by Internet world (data harvesting) • New technology – based on name-value pars -Jason objects • https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/NoSQL • Coverts data into name-value pairs in strings • Can be stored in some databases as strings • Fast ingest

text: members and make sure that people’s views are considered. • Honesty • You should always be honest about what is going well and what is going badly in a project. Chapter 22 Project management 58 Class Activity Role-based scenario (Topic: People Management / Team dynamics) Case study: Individual motivation Chapter 22 Project management 60 Alice is a software project manager working in a company that develops alarm systems. This company wishes to enter the growing market of assistive technology to help elderly and disabled people live independently. Alice has been asked to lead a team of 6 developers than can develop new products based around the company’s alarm technology. Alice’s assistive technology project starts well. Good working relationships develop within the team and creative new ideas are developed. The team decides to develop a peer-to- peer messaging system using digital televisions linked to the alarm network for communications. However, some months into the project, Alice notices that Dorothy, a hardware design expert, starts coming into work late, the quality of her work deteriorates and, increasingly, that she does not appear to be communicating with other members of the team. Alice talks about the problem informally with other team members to try to find out if Dorothy’s personal circumstances have changed, and if this might be affecting her work. They don’t know of anything, so Alice decides to talk with Dorothy to try to understand the problem. Case study: Individual motivation Chapter 22 Project management 61 After some initial denials that there is a problem, Dorothy admits that she has lost interest in the job. She expected that she would be able to develop and use her hardware interfacing skills. However, because of the product direction that has been chosen, she has little opportunity for this. Basically, she is working as a C programmer with other team members. Although she admits that the work is challenging, she is concerned that she is not developing her interfacing skills. She is worried that finding a job that involves hardware interfacing will be difficult after this project. Because she does not want to upset the team by revealing that she is thinking about the next project, she has decided that it is best to minimize conversation with them. Discussion • What do you think about Alice’s actions? • What do you think about Dorothy’s actions? • In general what do you think about the whole situation? Comments on case study • If you don’t sort out the problem of unacceptable work, the other group members will become dissatisfied and feel that they are doing an unfair share of the work. • Personal difficulties affect motivation because people can’t concentrate on their work. They need time and support to resolve these issues, although you have to make clear that they still have a responsibility to their employer. • Alice gives Dorothy more design autonomy and organizes training courses in software engineering that will give her more opportunities after her current project has finished. chunk_id: SFSU CSC648-848 [02] Spring 2026 C5 Usability GUI Principles Design Dev and Eval-0
text: Software Engineering CSC648/848 Henry VILLAR GUI PRINCIPLES Design, Development and Evaluation SW Engineering Process Components 1. Requirements and Specification 2. Design and Implementation 3. Validation 4. Maintenance and Evolution Usability & GUI Principles • Usability can be described as the capacity of a system to provide a condition for its users to perform the tasks: • Safely • Effectively • Efficiently • While enjoying the experience • In software engineering, usability is the degree to which a software can be used by specified consumers to achieve quantified objectives with effectiveness, efficiency, and satisfaction in a quantified context of use. • GUI Principles: General principles which guide UI design patterns • Design Patterns: User Interface Design patterns are recurring solutions that solve common design problems. Design patterns are standard reference points for the experienced user interface designer. • Development process: Personas>Functions>Mockups>h orizontal prototype>coding • UCD Copyright D. Petkovic 5 6 USER EXPERIENCE UX GUI 7 User Experience – more than GUI • User experience refers to ease of use of the product as a whole, and includes GUI but also everything else that “touches” the user: • Documentation • Installation • Purchase, registration • Support • API and SW tools usability • For non-SW products: • Packaging • Assembly • Ergonomics (physical) • …. Important Your goal – total user satisfaction • Make systems/sw that are all three: • Useful: A product which is useful is one that allows a user to accomplish a task or objective. • Usable. Usable refers to the usability of a given product. It is more than “useful” it examines the way that the product will be used and whether it enables the user to do so in a pleasurable, simple (or as simple as possible) and effective manner. • Used. A product may be both useful and usable and still fail to be used. The ultimate aim of a design is not to be useful or usable but for users to use that design. Without users a product is a failure and it doesn’t matter how great the design was – it’s still a failure. Driven by users not SW engineers 8 Important Always think of a USER • Who will use my works (SW, app, also documents - recall M submission instructions…) • What do they expect/know • What are their “characteristics” and background • What is the context of the usage • What will they want to do Lead to personas and use cases then to functions and design 9 Important Practical GUI development process • Develop personas (typical users) and use cases (OK to be a bit more detailed with UI design) • Define and prioritize functions • Create paper B&W mockups (NO programming) • Review mockups with internal users or domain experts and developers (internal focus groups) – revise revise revise with users; use GUI design principles • Create (horizontal) prototype – clickable UI no back end • Review mockups with users or domain experts and developers (interval focus groups) – revise revise revise with users (focus groups) ; use GUI design principles and patterns • Start coding • Get feedback, revise revise revise with users (focus groups); use established frameworks and stick to GUI design principles • Show to wider audience including limited external users 10 Important Iterate and get

text: with users; use GUI design principles • Create (horizontal) prototype – clickable UI no back end • Review mockups with users or domain experts and developers (interval focus groups) – revise revise revise with users (focus groups) ; use GUI design principles and patterns • Start coding • Get feedback, revise revise revise with users (focus groups); use established frameworks and stick to GUI design principles • Show to wider audience including limited external users 10 Important Iterate and get feedback from users Use cases for UX and UI • Can be a bit more details as necessary to guide UI design • Can be “mini-use cases” focusing on specific UI widgets in detail (slider vs. round dial, VCR play buttons vs. regular buttons) • Should be discussed and reviewed by users (off-line or focus groups) • Use for evaluation of mockups and UI working code 11 12 User Characteristics Know your user, client, customer!!!! • First, some general observations about the user • How people interact with computers • Human characteristics of relevance to GUI Important 13 Important Human Characteristics related to GUI: Perception Awareness and understanding of elements and objects through sight, sound…. Influenced in part by experience • Proximity: objects relate to each other • Similarity: similar objects should relate • Simple designs are easier to remember • Closure, unity: objects forming closed shape perceived as groups • Balance and symmetry: vertical and horizontal • Subjective perception of motion, image, video,audio quality • Context: color backgrounds influence color perception etc. ➔Implications for GUI screen and element design (to be covered later) ➔Drives GUI design patterns Important 14 Important Human Characteristics related to GUI: Movement Control- Fitts’ Law • Keyboard, mouse, trackball usage involves movement • Fitt’s Law (1954) is one of the rare exact laws related to usability: Time-to-acquire-target = a + b*Log2(2A/W) (A is distance, W is width of the target) ➔ • Large objects for important functions • Group related buttons • Pining action of the sides, top, bottom and corners of screens • Avoid small clickable objects Important 15 Important Human Characteristics related to GUI: Interactivity Response Time User reaction and perception < 0.1 sec. Instantaneous, no interruptions < 1.0 sec. Delay noticed, but no break in thought stream > 10.sec Interruption, user switches to another task Important 16 User/task considerations (for categorizing and surveying users) Knowledge and Experience • Computer Literacy • System experience • Application experience • Task experience • Education • Reading level • Typing skill • Native language or Culture Important 17 User/task considerations (for categorizing and surveying users) Job/task/Need • Type of system use • Frequency of use • Task importance • Task structure • Social interactions • Training • Turnover rate • Job category • Lifestyle Important 18 User/task considerations (for categorizing and surveying users) Psychological Characteristics re: job/task • Attitude • Motivation • Patience • Expectations • Stress level • Cognitive style Important 19 User/task considerations (for categorizing and surveying users) Physical characteristics • Age • Gender • Handedness • Disabilities Important 20 Human interaction speeds • Reading: paper 200 w/minute; computer 180 w/min • Listening: 150 w/minute • Typing (classic): 60 w/minute average • Keyboard typing: transcripts 33 w/minute; composition 19 words/minute • Hand printing: memorized text 31 w/minute; copying 22 w/minute • Expects system response

To be felt fully interactive UI Design patterns • UI design patterns follow UI principles – they offer practical designs guidance (and sometimes code) for common problems (registration, sequences, search, forms…) • Major companies issuing their design patterns and guidance with tools (Apple, Google…) • Analogy to architectural patterns • Design Patterns: • Getting Input (Forms, Explaining the process, community driven) • Navigation (Tabs, Jumping hierarchy, menus, content) • Dealing with Data (tables, formatting data, images, search) • Social (Reputation, interaction) 21 GUI Design Patterns – implementation issues • UX Designers and vendors publish design patterns and tools • Use and leverage them 22 Apple and Google UI design principles and guidance ➔ GUI design patterns and tools • Apple • https://developer.apple.com/library/ios/documentatio n/UserExperience/Conceptual/MobileHIG/ • https://developer.apple.com/design/tips/ • Google • http://developer.android.com/design/get- started/principles.html 23 24 LOGO TAB1 TAB2 TAB 3 Sign in/register Categories filters, Search bar always there MAIN AREA KEY CONTENT RESULTS ACTIONS sponsored Privacy, contact “Vanilla” WWW pages layout More to less important Important 25 Requirements Conceptual design, prototype, Specs, style guide High-level design, Prototype, specs Low-level design, Prototype, specs Code and unit test System test Req. met? Plan EVALUATE (users, management, Support, sales, QA…) Done! User-Centered Design and Development – MUST DO for GUI http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/User-centered_design Important 26 High level Principles of User Interface Design • General principles which guide UI design patterns. • Derived from inherent cognitive/perceptual human properties • List given in alphabetical not priority order • All principles are important, BUT relative importance might depend on users and business objectives Have this as a check list to design/evaluate GUIs Important 27 How do you rank importance of GUI design principles? • Aesthetically pleasing • Clarity • Compatibility • Easy to understand • Configurability • Consistency • Control • Context • Efficiency • Familiarity • Functionality • Forgiveness, recovery • Predictability • Recovery • Responsiveness • Simplicity • Transparency • Design trade-offs https://www.figma.com/resource-library/ui-design-principles/ (7) 28 My top: •Simplicity • Control • Aesthetically pleasing • Functionality • Context https://xd.adobe.com/ideas/process/ui-design/4-golden-rules-ui-design/ Focus groups - Essential tool in developing requirements in UCD • http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Focus_group • “In usability engineering, a focus group is a survey method to collect the views of users on software or a website. This marketing method can be applied to computer products to better understand the motivations of users and their perception of the product. Unlike other methods of ergonomics, focus group implies several participants: users or future users of the application. The focus group can only collect subjective data, not objective data on the use of the application as the usability test for example.” • Used for high level and also low level detailed feedback Important How to run focus groups Interaction Design Foundation (05/28/18) • https://www.interaction- design.org/literature/article/how-to-conduct-focus- groups • Choice of participants (recall your peronas) • Preparing • Conducting it • Note taking

chunk_id: SFSU CSC648-848 [02] Spring 2026 C6 SW Project Management Ethics and IP-0
text: Software Engineering CSC648/848 Henry VILLAR SW PROJECT MANAGEMENT SW PM, Ethics and IP 3 SW Project Management – Key goal Goal: Build and deliver the SW product that: 1. Satisfies specs (no more, no less) 2. It’s done on budget 3. It’s done on schedule 4. Delights the customers 5. Is maintainable, easy to sell, well supported, documented and marketed… “ Good is better than the best” Important 4 The project scheduling process •Copyright: Ian Sommerville Important Progress Meeting (Video): https://youtu.be/u8Kt7fRa2Wc 5 Task duration and definition • Initially, allocate at most weekly granularity for tasks, later use short duration (usually down to 2 days) • If task is more than 8 weeks, set a checkpoint/milestone in between • Initially have weekly check procedure • Progress report to manager, rolled up • Meeting • More often would add too more overhead • Exceptions in time of crises, resolving risks, close to delivery • Even daily meetings can be effective if done properly • Report on progress by all (go around the table): • On track or not, issues, risks Important 6 Duration and resource estimation • Usually provided by SW developers or tech leads • Various people have different ability to estimate: some always underestimate. Know this and readjust for each person • Use previous experience (good or bad) • Do not assume everything will be fine • Principal resource in SW development is human time • The more novel, the more risky ➔ readjust the schedule • Do not forget unit testing, integration testing and documentation • Rule of thumb: whatever people tell you, multiply with 1.3 or 1.5 safety factor Important 7 Mythical man-month syndrome • Coined by Brooks (at times where mostly men were doing SW) • Adding people late in the project is counterproductive: people training and integration effort is most often more than the original delay • Think how long it takes to get a new person to be productive – easily 2 months… • Develop the job adv. and get management approvals • Advertise • Collect resumes and evaluate • Interview • Bring on board • Train Important 8 Ways to present project schedules • Bar charts • Activity networks • Gantt chart (e.g. implemented in Microsoft Project) Can be done in simple document editors or special tools (Simple, no special tools is OK and desired) Important 9 Task durations and dependencies •Copyright: Ian Sommerville Important Task Effort (person-days) Duration (days) Dependencies T1 15 10 T2 8 15 T3 20 15 T1 (M1) T4 5 10 T5 5 10 T2, T4 (M3) T6 10 5 T1, T2 (M4) T7 25 20 T1 (M1) T8 75 25 T4 (M2) T9 10 15 T3, T6 (M5) T10 20 15 T7, T8 (M6) T11 10 10 T9 (M7) T12 20 10 T10, T11 (M8) Activity bar chart (Gantt chart) 10/12/2014 Chapter 23 Project Planning 10 Important Staff allocation chart 10/12/2014 Chapter 23 Project Planning 11 Important 12 Activity network start T2 M3 T6 Finish T10 M7 T5 T7 M2 T4 M5 T8 4/7/99 8 days 14/7/99 15 days 4/8/99 15 days 25/8/99 7 days 5/9/99 10 days 19/9/99 15 days 11/8/99 25 days 10 days 20 days 5 days 25/7/99 15 days 25/7/99 18/7/99 10 days T1 M1 T3 T9 M6 T11 M8 T12 M4 •Copyright: Ian Sommerville

text: chart) 10/12/2014 Chapter 23 Project Planning 10 Important Staff allocation chart 10/12/2014 Chapter 23 Project Planning 11 Important 12 Activity network start T2 M3 T6 Finish T10 M7 T5 T7 M2 T4 M5 T8 4/7/99 8 days 14/7/99 15 days 4/8/99 15 days 25/8/99 7 days 5/9/99 10 days 19/9/99 15 days 11/8/99 25 days 10 days 20 days 5 days 25/7/99 15 days 25/7/99 18/7/99 10 days T1 M1 T3 T9 M6 T11 M8 T12 M4 •Copyright: Ian Sommerville Critical path Important 13 Dependencies • Often, for one task to start, one or more tasks have to be completed first ➔ dependencies • Milestones have multiple task dependencies ➔ all those tasks have to be completed before milestone can be reached • Critical path: the longest path in the activity charts ➔ it determines the duration of the projects • Delays in critical path task cause delay in the project • Delays in non-critical path tasks may be tolerated as log as they are less than critical path Important Issues with SW Requirements • Seldom in writing, often verbal • Vague, many implicit ones • May be conflicting • Change a lot (for good and bad reasons) • Hard to know/understand/comprehend all of them up front • … Important Planning, cost estimation, PM • Usually, 90% of the cost is people • Programmer productivity can vary 10 X • Great deal of people management needed • Adding people does not help (even can create problems - Mythical man-month” by Brooks) • Very difficult to monitor progress • BUT: SCOPE can be changed even at the end (e.g. drop a function) Important 16 Tasks of SW Manager • Development of product vision and goals, and communicating them • Develop a Plan – schedules, budget, resource needs and what is delivered (for the team and for the management) • Execute, manage to successful delivery • Proposal writing and presentations • Funding and budget management • Planning • Scheduling • Costing • Providing adequate resources • Dealing with all stakeholders inside and outside the company • Monitoring and tracking • Reviews • Help resolving technical conflicts • Resolving personality conflicts • Develop vs. buy vs. vend out decisions • Report writing and presentations • Dealing with global development Important 17 SW Manager SW developers SW tech leads Peers (marketing, Sales, docs) Peers: business dev, HR, finance Upper management Subcontractors Clients, customers Multiple communication Needed for SW management Important 18 Project planning • Project planning is iterative, ongoing activity: • Start form best plan you can get • Iterate, change, adjust often (more often in times of crisis or delivery) based on ALL pertinent information • Technical • Internal (progress of the team etc.) • External (changes in user requirements, competition, market) • Keep overall goals in mind (delivery, schedules, budget and users) • Drastic changes in plan need to be approved and communicated (I.e. schedules are there to me met) • Remember: requirements change, and it is hard to monitor progress, so be nimble and iterative and check/revise often ➔ adopt agile SE processes Important 19 Cost (people, resources) Quality Time (schedule) 3 TRADITIONAL LEVERS TO PLAY WITH SCOPE (features) Unique to SW Variables in planning - they are related (E.g. if you want more features It either requires more people OR

chunk_id: SFSU CSC648-848 [02] Spring 2026 C6 SW Project Management Ethics and IP-2
text: • Drastic changes in plan need to be approved and communicated (I.e. schedules are there to me met) • Remember: requirements change, and it is hard to monitor progress, so be nimble and iterative and check/revise often ➔ adopt agile SE processes Important 19 Cost (people, resources) Quality Time (schedule) 3 TRADITIONAL LEVERS TO PLAY WITH SCOPE (features) Unique to SW Variables in planning - they are related (E.g. if you want more features It either requires more people OR more time OR quality needs to be reduced) Important 20 Vertical vs. matrix management • Vertical: all resources are in one department. • Pros: easier to organize • Cons: duplication, not optimal use of resources (I.e. repeated UI development team for each product line) • Matrix: Resources marshaled on per project basis from different departments • Pros: better use of resources, optimal talent used • Cons: harder to manage and organize. Critical to have cooperation between project manager in charge of project and other managers contributing resources Important 21 Checkpoints, Milestones and deliverables • SW is hard to “touch and see”, it is intangible ➔ some verifiable and well defined milestones are needed for monitoring the progress • Checkpoint: a defined time you will check some specific smaller issue (used internally to resolve technical or other problems) • Milestone: end point of some logically distinct and defined activity: report, mockups, algorithmic solution, DB connected to UI etc. • Deliverable: milestone that is delivered to somebody outside of the project, can be verified and measured. Often has contractual importance. • Deliverable is also a milestone, but milestone may not be a deliverable • M1-4 are milestones, M5 is deliverable • Spread milestones/checkpoints in time, at least every 2-3 weeks) Important 22 Task 1 ************************* Task 2 ********** Task 3 ********* Jan Feb March April Simple way to do Gantt chart – no tools 23 Revisions/Optimization • Task scheduling and people allocation can be modified in some cases to reduce critical path or costs • Perform activities in parallel (need people) • Serialize non critical activities as long as critical path is the same (save people) 24 ***************** ************* ****** John John Mary ***************** ************ ****** John Peter Mary Less people, More time More people, Less time T1 T2 T3 T1 T2 T3 Time (Can do it if T1 and T2 are independent) 25 1) Use project requirements and design documents (tasks, UI, use cases, architecture) + budget, resources and completion date to get basic info and project parameters and constraints (I.e. Final Project M1 and M2) 2) Define project tasks, milestones and deliverables (variables: cost, time, quality, scope) 3) Define dependencies 4) Estimate task duration 5) Estimate task resources (people, equipment, SW, HW) 6) Draw up project schedule (I.e. backproject from desired completion date. IF big changes needed go back to Step 1) 7) Review periodically (more often toward the end of the project or when risks are identified) 8) Revise as necessary 9) IF problems arise initiate review 10) Loop back to Step 6 until project done Summary: Steps in project management Only if critical problems arise Important 26 SE ethics • You are the key developer of a SW for a medical system, the SW has to be safe and reliable, patient lives are at risk • Your boss

text: Step 1) 7) Review periodically (more often toward the end of the project or when risks are identified) 8) Revise as necessary 9) IF problems arise initiate review 10) Loop back to Step 6 until project done Summary: Steps in project management Only if critical problems arise Important 26 SE ethics • You are the key developer of a SW for a medical system, the SW has to be safe and reliable, patient lives are at risk • Your boss asks for tight schedule, pushes aggressively for project to be completed • You worry that the resulting SW is not safe for patients • You are afraid you might be fired if you resist your boss • What do you do? ➔ Apply SE ethics 27 SE ethics • What to do? • Refuse to compromise • Compromise • “Blow the whistle” • Difficult, and legally not fully regulated for now Ethical AI – BIG topic of today • AI is becoming part of many critical applications in health, military business and everyday life (driverless cars, loan applications) • BUT: • Do we know HOW it works (Explainability) • Is it tested well, is it reproducible and verifiable • Is it designed ethically to be safe and non-discriminatory • If it causes problems who to complain to, can you sue for damages (and who to sue) • What do you do as developer and tester of AI systems 28 Readings on AI Ethics – really important stuff to read and know – big topic • AI Principles – Future of Life Institute • https://futureoflife.org/ai-principles/ • Upcoming Workshop on AI Ethics in Biomedicine, D. Petkovic et al, PSB 2020 • https://psb.stanford.edu/workshop/ai/ • EU General Data Protection Initiative (GDPR) • https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/General_Data_Protection_Regulation 29 30 SE Ethics and IEEE/ACM Society • IEEE Computer Society and ACM approved SE code of ethics 1999: • https://www.acm.org/about-acm/code-of-ethics • Still legally not binding 31 1. PUBLIC - Software engineers shall act consistently with the public interest. 2. CLIENT AND EMPLOYER - Software engineers shall act in a manner that is in the best interests of their client and employer consistent with the public interest. 3. PRODUCT - Software engineers shall ensure that their products and related modifications meet the highest professional standards possible. 4. JUDGMENT - Software engineers shall maintain integrity and independence in their professional judgment. 5. MANAGEMENT - Software engineering managers and leaders shall subscribe to and promote an ethical approach to the management of software development and maintenance. 6. PROFESSION - Software engineers shall advance the integrity and reputation of the profession consistent with the public interest. 7. COLLEAGUES - Software engineers shall be fair to and supportive of their colleagues. 8. SELF - Software engineers shall participate in lifelong learning regarding the practice of their profession and shall promote an ethical approach to the practice of the profession. distribute. • http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Digital_library#Copyright_and_licensing • http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Licensing 35 Trademarks • Used to protect distinct logo, sign, word, phrase specific to business or service • Can be registered or unregistered • See http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trademarks Important 36 Patents • Protect an idea, not particular implementation or embodiments • Covers for e.g. algorithm, methods, processes, medical drugs • Granted for period of time to inventors/organizations • Must be applied for • Check http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Patent • Familiarize yourself with how patent looks https://patents.google.com/patent/US6182069 Important 37 Trade secrets • Method, formula, process kept secret by the company (and not patented) to offer it competitive advantage (note: patents expire after some time). • Coca Cola • Check http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trade_secrets Important 38 SW licensing • SW license is legal way of covering its distribution (different from copyright which covers the ownership/authorship) • Commercial e.g. when purchasing SW (EULA’s – end user licensing agreement) • Open Source allow free distribution (BUT check for details – is the SW which uses Open Source also required to be free?) • Check http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Software_license Important 39 IT usage and SW licensing at SFSU • Familiarize yourself: • http://cs.sfsu.edu/student-software-licensing-policies • http://cs.sfsu.edu/it-resources-acceptable-use-policy Important Team Project Time • M2 More Detailed Requirements, Specs, Architecture, UI mock-ups and Vertical SW prototype - Two goals: • More detailed design - Milestone 2 document: prioritize requirements; design high level UI mockups and storyboards; design high level DB organization, and practice UML. chunk_id: SFSU CSC648-848 [02] Spring 2026 C6 SW Project Management Ethics and IP-5
text: first simple bare-bones prototype (from DB to browser) to test the infrastructure, educate the team, resolve technical issues and also serve as basis of further development (must run on the deployment server). (one per team) to be reviewed in class and also by Class CTO (off-line) Study Materials 42 SW Management - Wikipedia • http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Software_project_management SW management is also (from Wikipedia link above): • Risk management is the process of measuring or assessing risk and then developing strategies to manage the risk. plan. • Requirements management is the process of identifying, eliciting, documenting, analyzing, tracing, prioritizing and agreeing on requirements and then controlling change and communicating to relevant stakeholders. • Change management is the process of identifying, documenting, analyzing, prioritizing and agreeing on changes to scope (project management) and then controlling changes and communicating to relevant stakeholders. • Software configuration management is the process of identifying, and documenting the scope itself, which is the software product underway, including all sub-products and changes and enabling communication of these to relevant stakeholders. • Release management is the process of identifying, documenting, prioritizing and agreeing on releases of software and then controlling the release schedule and communicating to relevant stakeholders 43 Milestones and deliverables: examples • 80% complete is not a good milestone – hard to verify and measure for SW projects • Good ones: • Architecture agreed upon • Data access designed, implemented and unit tested • UI mockups done • Check that Google maps can be integrated in our system – throw away prototype • DB connected with front end, works according to specs • Interface defined, unit tested and implemented • User study done • Images scanned and imported • Beta delivered to customers for evaluation 44 Scheduling – it is hard… • SW in intangible with progress hard to monitor (vs. building a house) • Technical uncertainties • Great variation in programmer productivity (over 10 X) (vs. say windows installers) • Hard to reuse previous examples (but OK as a starting point) • Tools and technology change • Different people have different productivity • Risk with use of latest technology (teams needs to learn, bugs, incomplete features) • Unanticipated changes: customer requests, requirements, business, market, competition Very difficult and demanding ➔ requires experience and constant iterations and reevaluation 45 SW Project Management - resources • List of some good resources (many available) • http://www.rspa.com/spi/project-mgmt.html#resources • Lately, a lot of tools emerged to help manage tasks list in a teams setting, e.g. • Trello • https://trello.com/ Important for class team leads • For every task you assign have a person responsible and the deadline • For every person have a task • Check progress around middle of the time line • Deadlines are to be respected! • Use task management systems that offer shared dashboard (e.g. Trello) 46 47 Trello 48 Case study • You are PM for a startup which developed a new product • You developed a new logo for the company • You developed novel alg. for search/data mining that is your key advantage • You will develop APIs to distribute and sell • You need to use some digital images made by others for your WWW site • Question: What IP protections you need for each? 49 Review Questions • Why is SW hard to manage

chunk_id: SFSU CSC648-848 [02] Spring 2026 C6 SW Project Management Ethics and IP-6
text: 48 Case study • You are PM for a startup which developed a new product • You developed a new logo for the company • You developed novel alg. for search/data mining that is your key advantage • You will develop APIs to distribute and sell • You need to use some digital images made by others for your WWW site • Question: What IP protections you need for each? 49 Review Questions • Why is SW hard to manage and why is it unique • What are some of the typical tasks for SW manager • What is the importance of people management in SW, and why • If your SW is built by many teams globally located, what are some issues you have to pay particular attention in terms of final delivery, management of the whole process etc. • What are variable a typical SW manager can work with in scheduling and planning 50 Review Questions • Vertical vs. matrix management • What are milestones and what are deliverables • What is critical path • Draw typical bar charts, activity diagrams, Gantt charts • What are typical organizations of SW teams • Go over cases given in the class slides and think what would you do as manager, manager’s manager or SW developer • Know SE ethics principles as approved by IEEE/ACM • Know various IP issues and what works in what acse 51 Summary • Issues that need utmost care: • High level project management, organization and coordination • Centralized decisions and coordination for key high level issues (i.e. requirements,specs, architecture) • Communication: teamwork, trust, written and verbal communication, conflict resolution • Culture • People management • Tools: for communication, project management, SW development • Technical management: • Task allocation vs. modular architecture, well defined interfaces and functions etc. • Source control and management • QA and integration testing • Lower-level project management including risk management • Encouraging best ideas and innovation while promoting teamwork Thank You

text: --- Slide 1 --- Software Engineering CSC648/848 --- Slide 2 --- Henry VILLAR Coding STYLE and Coding Best Practices --- Slide 3 --- Objective Define Coding Style Analyze Benefits Best Practices for: Formatting Style Documentation Coding for security References to use (often language specific) – note: tools/standards change in time How to enforce coding style --- Slide 4 --- What is this about? --- Slide 5 --- Now you know – so style and formatting helps --- Slide 6 --- Why coding style? Most time in SW lifecycle is spend on the maintenance phase This is mostly done by HUMANS  code readability helps Most time you maintain code of somebody else (and not easy to contact) Also: Ask yourself if you understand the code YOU wrote months ago… Important What is it? Programming style, also known as code style, is a set of rules or guidelines used when writing the source code for a computer program. It is often claimed that following a particular programming style will help programmers read and understand source code conforming to the style, and help to avoid introducing errors. Wikipedia --- Slide 7 --- SW Engineering Process Components (Lifecycle) --- Slide 8 --- SW Engineering Process Components (Lifecycle) --- Slide 9 --- Elements of coding style Naming Conventions (variables, functions, classes) File Naming and Organization Code Formatting Code Comments Documentation Coding practices Best practices in how to code e.g. for security Important --- Slide 10 --- Benefits of good coding style Easier maintenance (big thing) Easier testing Easier optimization Easier portability Why coding style and benefits https://www.multidots.com/importance-of-code-quality-and-coding-standard-in-software-development/ Important --- Slide 11 --- Specific coding styles…. Often depend on languages Ton of good coding styles adopted and became de-facto standard Teams have to agree on coding style consistent with the company policy and best practices Standards change – follow them Enforce coding style by coding review (No coding style used  no submission!!!) Brainstorm with your team and list your identified risks (skills, schedule, technical, etc.) Project Management Assign tasks and describe how your team is working together. --- Slide 25 --- M2 – Vertical Prototype Vertical prototype [1] shall allow one to enter a search term on test home page (simple home page used to test vertical prototype), [2] then get a response form the DB and render it back on the browser in a simple test result page. UI for the test home page can be a simple one containing Class, section and team identification One free text entry field (this text is then used in SQL %LIKE search – check architecture) One pull down for 2-3 search parameter (search category like student or professional, major, roommate gender, room rent, etc.) – this is exercised using simple SQL filter – see architecture slides The test result page needs to display search results including images and later their thumbnails as well as profile videos (if implemented) in any reasonable layout (the goal here is to make sure you can access and display items and NOT the ultimate UI). Thumbnails need to be computed automatically using some open source thumbnail generation SW – you can do thumbnails later after you figure out the basic way to show imageThe DB can have only a few items. text: CSC 648/848 Software Engineering – Fall 2024 Milestone 3 Review of functionality, UI, SW and planning for final product delivery Includes Checklist for instructors and teams (Appendix I and II) Henry Villar, CEO & CTO, SFSU jvillar@mail.sfsu.edu Acknowledge Content based on class material created by Dr. D. Petkovic & Anthony Souza Objective and Overview The objectives of Milestone 3 are to: - Define exactly what product you are delivering. We will come to agreement on what the final application is going to look like in terms of functionality, especially which functional items are priority 1 (P1). This will be your commitment to the instructor/client. - Ensure software development is on track. We will verify that all the SW components are installed and integrated and that most major functions work. The software you present must follow adopted coding style, be reasonably documented and deployed on the final deployment platform. Github usage (organization, comments to postings etc.) will also be checked. - Provide feedback on all major UI screens and functionality. This will be done through a review of the so-called horizontal or UI prototype (see below). - Check the code and software architecture by reviewing your code, the database and overall design at a high level (may be done partially off-line, remotely) - Check all algorithms like search and filtering - Ensure basic best practices for security are followed - Identify and address all technical risks - Ensure effective teamwork by verifying that all team members have started implementation of their portion of the project - Ensure software development is effective by verifying your team’s coding practices, software engineering practices and usage of team development tools Milestone 3 will be in the form of two part review: 1. Part 1 – each team meets with Prof. Villar Review of functionality, UI and general project status (in person) will be done by each team meeting Prof. Villar for 20 – 30 minutes. in person (zoom breakout room). In order to be efficient teams must observe strict schedule and come fully prepared. Checklist for Part 1 review is in Appendix I 2. Part 2: SW review (off-line): Architecture, Code, github, team account, database and general SW and code review will be done remotely by the instructor one day after Part 1 review (and partially in Part 1 by Prof. Villar). The Instructor/Grader will fill out a report (Appendix II) and send to each team After Milestone 3 the teams will meet to analyze feedback and revise design and implementation accordingly, as well as to fix on P1 set of features. After this the teams will have “feature freeze” e.g. the teams must focus on intense implementation of only P1 features. Part 1 Review – Functionality and UI feedback and general project status IMPORTANT! BE ON TIME (see schedule below). Schedule will be strictly enforced Appoint a “scribe” e.g. person to collect meeting feedback and main points and action items (use Appendix I as a template). You can also record the feedback on video nut you MUST NOT post it on any media Bring all required items – failure to do so will reduce team grade Have your laptops ready, tested and batteries full. Have a backup laptop You have only a limited time available for the meeting. Any time you lose due to

Review Schedule and meeting place (zoom/in-person class): TBD and will be strictly observed In terms of attendance, all team members need to be present. What to bring to the meeting?
